<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define a data frame in R. List any four characteristics of a data frame.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A data frame is a two-dimensional, rectangular data structure in R used to store tabular data, where each column is a vector of homogeneous type and all columns have the same length. (2 marks)

Four characteristics: (½ × 4 = 2 marks, but space allows 4 key points)

1. **Two-dimensional** — has rows and columns.
2. **Heterogeneous columns** — different columns can hold different types.
3. **Homogeneous within column** — a single column contains values of the same type.
4. **Equal length columns** — all columns must have the same number of rows.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between a matrix and a data frame in R.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Matrix | Data Frame |
|---------|--------|------------|
| Data type | Homogeneous (single type) | Heterogeneous (different types per column allowed) |
| Dimensions | 2-D | 2-D |
| Function to create | `matrix()` | `data.frame()` |
| Use case | Mathematical operations | Tabular/real-world data |
| Internal structure | Atomic vector with dim attribute | List of equal-length vectors |

(1 mark per valid point × 3 = 3 marks; comparison style usually gives 3 differences for full marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of a data frame in R. Discuss any **four** different methods to create a data frame with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A data frame is a two-dimensional, heterogeneous, tabular data structure in R. Each column is a vector of the same data type, but different columns can have different types. All columns must have equal length. (2 marks)

**Four methods to create a data frame:**

**Method 1: Using `data.frame()` from vectors** (2 marks)

```r
Name <- c("A", "B", "C")
Age  <- c(20, 21, 22)
df1 <- data.frame(Name, Age, stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
```

**Method 2: Using `data.frame()` with inline vectors** (1 mark)

```r
df2 <- data.frame(
  Name  = c("A", "B", "C"),
  Age   = c(20, 21, 22),
  Pass  = c(TRUE, TRUE, FALSE),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
```

**Method 3: Using `read.csv()` to load external data** (1 mark)

```r
df3 <- read.csv("students.csv")
```

**Method 4: Converting a list to a data frame using `as.data.frame()`** (1 mark)

```r
lst <- list(Name=c("A","B"), Age=c(20,21))
df4 <- as.data.frame(lst)
```

**(b)** Consider the following data frame:

```r
emp <- data.frame(
  EmpID  = c(101, 102, 103, 104, 105),
  Name   = c("Arun", "Maya", "Rahul", "Sneha", "Kiran"),
  Salary = c(50000, 60000, 55000, 70000, 48000),
  Dept   = c("IT", "HR", "IT", "Finance", "HR"),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
```

Write R code to perform the following operations: **(7 marks)**

1. Display the structure and dimensions of `emp`. (1 mark)
2. Display the names of all employees working in the **IT** department. (2 marks)
3. Add a new column `Bonus` which is **10% of Salary**. (1 mark)
4. Sort the data frame by `Salary` in **descending order**. (1 mark)
5. Find the **average salary** of employees in the **HR** department. (2 marks)

**Model Solution:**

```r
# 1. Structure and dimensions
str(emp)
dim(emp)
```

```r
# 2. IT department employees
emp[emp$Dept == "IT", "Name"]
# or equivalently:
emp$Name[emp$Dept == "IT"]
```

**Logic:** `emp$Dept == "IT"` returns a logical vector; used as row index, returns only matching rows; `"Name"` selects only the Name column.

```r
# 3. Add Bonus column (10% of Salary)
emp$Bonus <- emp$Salary * 0.10
```

**Logic:** Vectorized operation — `emp$Salary * 0.10` produces a new vector, which is assigned as the `Bonus` column.

```r
# 4. Sort by Salary descending
emp <- emp[order(-emp$Salary), ]
```

**Logic:** `order(-emp$Salary)` gives row indices in descending salary order; `[ , ]` reorders rows accordingly.

```r
# 5. Average salary of HR department
mean(emp$Salary[emp$Dept == "HR"])
```

**Logic:** `emp$Salary[emp$Dept == "HR"]` extracts salaries of HR employees; `mean()` computes the arithmetic mean.

**Expected Output (for verification):**
- IT Names: "Arun", "Rahul"
- Bonus values: 5000, 6000, 5500, 7000, 4800
- After sort, Kiran (48000) is first; Sneha (70000) is last
- HR Average: (60000 + 48000)/2 = **54000**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** What is a data frame in R? Explain the following operations on a data frame with examples: (i) Indexing, (ii) Adding and deleting rows and columns, (iii) Sorting. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A data frame is a 2-D, heterogeneous, tabular structure where each column is a vector of equal length. (1 mark)

**(i) Indexing** (2 marks)

Three forms of indexing:

```r
df$col              # dollar accessor — returns vector
df[ , "col"]        # matrix-style with name — returns vector
df[i, j]            # element access
df[cond, ]          # conditional row filtering
```

Example:
```r
df[1, 2]            # First row, second column
df[df$Age > 21, ]   # All rows where Age > 21
```

**(ii) Adding and Deleting Rows and Columns** (2 marks)

```r
# Add column
df$Bonus <- df$Salary * 0.1

# Add row
df <- rbind(df, data.frame(Name="X", Age=25, Salary=50000))

# Delete column
df$Bonus <- NULL

# Delete row
df <- df[-3, ]
```

**(iii) Sorting** (2 marks)

```r
# Ascending
df <- df[order(df$Age), ]

# Descending
df <- df[order(-df$Salary), ]

# Multiple keys
df <- df[order(df$Dept, -df$Salary), ]
```

**(b)** Consider the data frame `sales` with columns `Product`, `Quantity`, `Price`, `Region`. Write R code to: **(7 marks)**

1. Compute the **total revenue** (Quantity × Price) for each row and store it in a new column called `Revenue`. (2 marks)
2. Find the **product with the highest revenue**. (2 marks)
3. Compute the **average revenue per region**. (3 marks)

**Model Solution:**

```r
sales <- data.frame(
  Product  = c("A", "B", "C", "D", "E", "F"),
  Quantity = c(10, 5, 8, 12, 7, 9),
  Price    = c(100, 200, 150, 80, 300, 120),
  Region   = c("North", "South", "North", "East", "South", "East"),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

# 1. Total revenue per row
sales$Revenue <- sales$Quantity * sales$Price
```

**Logic:** Element-wise vectorized multiplication across two equal-length numeric vectors. (2 marks)

```r
# 2. Product with highest revenue
sales$Product[which.max(sales$Revenue)]
```

**Logic:** `which.max()` returns the **row index** of the maximum value in `Revenue`. Indexing `sales$Product` with that index returns the product name. (2 marks)

**Alternative:**
```r
sales[sales$Revenue == max(sales$Revenue), "Product"]
```

**Verification:** Revenues = 1000, 1000, 1200, 960, 2100, 1080. Maximum = 2100 (Product **E**).

```r
# 3. Average revenue per region
aggregate(Revenue ~ Region, data = sales, FUN = mean)
```

**Logic:** `aggregate()` splits `Revenue` by `Region` and applies `mean` to each group. (3 marks)

**Alternative using `tapply()`:**
```r
tapply(sales$Revenue, sales$Region, FUN = mean)
```

**Expected Output:**

| Region | Revenue (mean) |
|--------|----------------|
| East   | (960 + 1080)/2 = **1020** |
| North  | (1000 + 1200)/2 = **1100** |
| South  | (1000 + 2100)/2 = **1550** |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Data Frame Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting `stringsAsFactors = FALSE`** in older R versions → columns become factors → numeric operations fail → **lose 1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing `df[i, j]` with `df[i][j]`** — the latter does *not* work as expected on data frames.
> 3. **Writing `mean(df$Dept == "HR")` instead of `mean(df$Salary[df$Dept == "HR"])`** — wrong quantity averaged → **lose 1-2 marks**.
> 4. **Mixing up `rbind()` and `cbind()`** — `rbind` adds rows, `cbind` adds columns.
> 5. **Not preserving original data frame when deleting** — students often write `df$col <- NULL` correctly but forget to **reassign** if they used `df[-i, -j]` style.
> 6. **Missing the comma in `df[ , j]`** → returns a data frame instead of a vector → errors in vectorized operations.
> 7. **Not mentioning `stringsAsFactors`** when discussing `data.frame()` creation → examiner deducts 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Data Frames:**
>
> - **Definition:** 2-D, rectangular, heterogeneous tabular structure; columns are equal-length vectors of possibly different types.
> - **Create:** `data.frame(col1, col2, ..., stringsAsFactors = FALSE)`.
> - **Inspect:** `str(df)`, `head(df)`, `tail(df)`, `summary(df)`, `dim(df)`, `class(df)`.
> - **Access column:** `df$col`, `df[["col"]]`, `df[ , "col"]`.
> - **Access row:** `df[i, ]`.
> - **Access cell:** `df[i, j]`.
> - **Filter:** `df[cond, ]` where `cond` is a logical vector.
> - **Add column:** `df$newcol <- values`.
> - **Add row:** `rbind(df, new_row)`.
> - **Delete column:** `df$col <- NULL`.
> - **Delete row:** `df[-i, ]`.
> - **Sort:** `df[order(df$col), ]` (ascending) or `df[order(-df$col), ]` (descending).
> - **Aggregate:** `aggregate(y ~ x, data=df, FUN=mean)` or `tapply(df$y, df$x, FUN=mean)`.
> - **Merge:** `merge(df1, df2, by="key")`.
> - **Read external CSV:** `read.csv("file.csv")` → returns a data frame.
> - **Key difference from matrix:** heterogeneous column types allowed.
> - **Key trap:** unequal-length vectors cause silent recycling or errors.
> - **Modern R (≥4.0):** `stringsAsFactors = FALSE` is the default, but **always specify it explicitly** in exam answers for safety and clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lists
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET862 - PROGRAMMING AND DATA ANALYTICS WITH R
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Lists

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **R programming language**, a **List** is a non-atomic, recursive, heterogeneous data structure that serves as the most flexible generic vector in R. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, a list is formally defined as:

> A **list** is an R object consisting of an ordered collection of objects (called *components* or *elements*) which can be of **different data types and different lengths**, including other lists, vectors, matrices, data frames, or functions.

Mathematically, a list $L$ of length $n$ can be represented as:

$$L = \{c_1, c_2, c_3, \dots, c_n\}$$

where each component $c_i$ can independently be:
- An atomic vector (numeric, character, logical, complex)
- Another list (recursive nesting)
- A matrix, array, or data frame
- A function or expression

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from Vectors**: A list is the only R data structure that can hold elements of **mixed data types and varying lengths simultaneously**. Atomic vectors are strictly homogeneous.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **college student's backpack** 🎒:

- The **backpack itself** is the *list* (the container).
- Inside it, there can be a **laptop** (numeric vector), a **notebook** (character vector), **pens of different colors** (logical values), a **smaller pouch with keys and coins** (a nested list), and a **calculator** (a function).

Just as the backpack happily holds all these *different* items in *different quantities* and *different shapes*, an R **list** allows you to bundle together any combination of data objects without forcing them into a uniform type.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Use in Engineering**: Lists are heavily used in R for storing outputs of statistical models (e.g., `lm()` returns a list containing coefficients, residuals, fitted values, and diagnostics), configuring machine learning pipelines, and managing API responses.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Properties

The following are the **canonical metrics** of an R list:

| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| **Type** | `list` |
| **Class** | `list` (by default) |
| **Recursion** | Yes (can contain other lists) |
| **Heterogeneity** | Fully supported |
| **Length Mutability** | Yes (can grow/shrink dynamically) |
| **Index Base** | 1 (R uses 1-based indexing) |

> [!TIP]
> The **`typeof()`** function returns `"list"` for any list object. The **`class()`** function returns `"list"` unless the list has been given a custom class (e.g., `"data.frame"`, `"lm"`).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why Lists Exist

R is built on the **S language** philosophy, which treats *everything* as an object and every operation as a function call. To accommodate the natural complexity of real-world data (where a single record might have a name, an age, scores across 5 subjects, and embedded metadata), R needed a **recursive container** that does **not flatten** its contents.

### The Three-Tier Logical Hierarchy

1. **Atomic Vector Tier** → Homogeneous, single-type data (`c(1,2,3)`)
2. **List Tier** → Heterogeneous, recursive container (the focus of this module)
3. **Data Frame Tier** → A special list of equal-length vectors (covered in Module 2)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight**: Technically, a *data frame* in R is internally stored as a **list of equal-length vectors**. This makes mastering lists a prerequisite for advanced data manipulation.

## 2.2 Core Operations on Lists (The 7 Pillars)

### Pillar 1: Creation
A list is created using the `list()` constructor function. Each argument becomes a named (or unnamed) component.

```r
# Unnamed list
my_list <- list(1, "hello", TRUE, c(1.5, 2.5))

# Named list (best practice)
student <- list(
  name = "Arjun",
  age = 21,
  grades = c(85, 90, 78),
  passed = TRUE
)
```

### Pillar 2: Length and Structure
- `length(L)` → Number of **top-level components**
- `str(L)` → Compact display of internal **structure**
- `names(L)` → Returns/assigns component names

### Pillar 3: Indexing (Three Operators)

| Operator | Example | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `[ ]` | `L[1]` | Returns a **list slice** (preserves list type) |
| `[[ ]]` | `L[[1]]` | Returns the **actual component** (extracts the object) |
| `$` | `L$name` | Returns component by **name** (sugar for `[[ ]]`) |

### Pillar 4: Modification
- **Add**: `L[[4]] <- "new"` or `L$new <- "new"`
- **Remove**: `L[[2]] <- NULL`
- **Concatenate**: `c(L1, L2)` or `append()`

### Pillar 5: Coercion and Conversion
- `unlist(L)` → Flattens list into a **vector** (with type coercion)
- `as.list(v)` → Converts vector to list
- `as.data.frame(L)` → Converts (when possible) to data frame

### Pillar 6: Applying Functions
- `lapply(L, FUN)` → Apply function over list, return **list**
- `sapply(L, FUN)` → Apply function, return **simplified** result (vector/matrix)
- `mapply(FUN, L1, L2)` → Multi-argument apply

### Pillar 7: Recursion and Nesting
Lists can contain lists, creating tree-like structures.

```r
nested <- list(
  outer1 = list(inner1 = 1, inner2 = 2),
  outer2 = "top"
)
nested$outer1$inner2  # Returns 2
```

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive reference** for the KTU 2024 ESE on Lists. Memorize this.

| Operation | R Syntax | Returns | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Create list | `list(a, b, c)` | A list object | Named if `name = value` used |
| Get length | `length(L)` | Integer | Top-level components only |
| Get names | `names(L)` | Character vector | NULL if unnamed |
| Index (type-preserving) | `L[i]` | Sub-list | Single `[ ]` keeps list class |
| Extract element | `L[[i]]` or `L$name` | The actual element | Use for computation |
| Add element | `L[[n+1]] <- x` | Updated list | Auto-extends list |
| Remove element | `L[[i]] <- NULL` | Updated list | NULL deletion |
| Concatenate | `c(L1, L2)` | Combined list | Top-level merge |
| Flatten | `unlist(L)` | Atomic vector | Coerces all to common type |
| Apply | `lapply(L, f)` | List of results | Always returns list |
| Simplified apply | `sapply(L, f)` | Vector/matrix | Simplifies if possible |
| Check type | `is.list(x)` | Logical | TRUE if list |
| Convert | `as.list(v)` | List | Atomic vector → list |

> [!WARNING]
> **The `[ ]` vs `[[ ]]` Pitfall**: This is the **#1 source of marks lost** in KTU exams. `L[1]` returns a list of length 1 containing the first element. `L[[1]]` returns the first element *itself*. Using the wrong one in a numeric operation will crash your code with `Error in ... : non-numeric argument to binary operator`.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Lists are the **backbone** of:
- **Statistical Modeling**: `result <- lm(y ~ x)` returns a list with 12+ components.
- **JSON Parsing**: The `jsonlite` package represents parsed JSON as nested lists.
- **Machine Learning**: `caret` model objects are lists with model metrics, parameters, and predictions.
- **Simulation**: Monte Carlo simulation results are commonly stored as lists of vectors/matrices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Creating and Manipulating a List

**Problem Statement (Model Example):** Create a list representing an IoT sensor reading that contains: (1) sensor ID, (2) timestamp, (3) a numeric vector of 5 readings, (4) a logical status flag, and (5) a nested list containing calibration parameters.

### Step 1: Construct the Outer List

```r
iot_sensor <- list(
  sensor_id    = "TEMP_SENSOR_07",
  timestamp    = "2026-03-04 14:32:00",
  readings     = c(22.5, 23.1, 22.8, 23.4, 22.9),
  is_active    = TRUE,
  calibration  = list(offset = 0.05, scale = 1.02, last_calibrated = "2026-01-15")
)
```

### Step 2: Verify Structure

```r
str(iot_sensor)
```

**Expected Output:**
```
List of 5
 $ sensor_id   : chr "TEMP_SENSOR_07"
 $ timestamp   : chr "2026-03-04 14:32:00"
 $ readings    : num [1:5] 22.5 23.1 22.8 23.4 22.9
 $ is_active   : logi TRUE
 $ calibration :List of 3
  ..$ offset         : num 0.05
  ..$ scale          : num 1.02
  ..$ last_calibrated: chr "2026-01-15"
```

### Step 3: Extract the Mean Reading

The mean reading calculation requires accessing the numeric vector using the `[[ ]]` operator (extraction), not `[ ]` (subsetting).

```r
mean_reading <- mean(iot_sensor[["readings"]])
# Equivalent: mean(iot_sensor$readings)

# Numerical evaluation:
# mean_reading = (22.5 + 23.1 + 22.8 + 23.4 + 22.9) / 5
```

Let us verify this step-by-step using the aligned math block:

$$
\text{Sum} = 22.5 + 23.1 + 22.8 + 23.4 + 22.9
$$

$$
\text{Sum} = 114.7
$$

$$
\text{mean\_reading} = \frac{\text{Sum}}{n} = \frac{114.7}{5} = 22.94
$$

So `mean_reading = 22.94` (°C).

### Step 4: Access Nested Element via Recursion

```r
# Access calibration offset (3 levels deep)
cal_offset <- iot_sensor$calibration$offset
# Result: 0.05
```

This works because:
- `iot_sensor$calibration` → returns the *inner list*
- Then `$offset` → extracts the numeric value `0.05`

### Step 5: Modify the List Dynamically

**Add a new component:**
```r
iot_sensor$location <- "Kerala, IN"
```
The list now has **6 components**.

**Remove the `is_active` flag:**
```r
iot_sensor$is_active <- NULL
```
The list reverts to **5 components**.

**Update a nested value:**
```r
iot_sensor$calibration$offset <- 0.07
```

### Step 6: Apply a Function Across the List

```r
# Get the length of each top-level component
component_lengths <- sapply(iot_sensor, length)
```

**Output:**
| Component | Length |
|---|---|
| sensor_id | 1 |
| timestamp | 1 |
| readings | 5 |
| calibration | 3 |
| location | 1 |

### Step 7: Convert to a Data Frame (Preview of Module 2)

```r
df <- as.data.frame(iot_sensor$readings)
```

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: `lapply` vs `sapply` vs `apply`

The following matrix compares the three primary list-iteration functions, which are heavily tested in KTU exams.

| Function | Input | Output | Simplification | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `lapply(X, FUN)` | List, vector | **List** | None (always list) | When output types vary |
| `sapply(X, FUN)` | List, vector | Vector / Matrix / List | Automatic | When output types are uniform |
| `apply(X, MARGIN, FUN)` | Matrix / Array | Vector / Array | Depends | Row/column operations on matrices |
| `mapply(FUN, ...)` | Multiple lists | List/vector | Optional | Multi-argument operations |

### Worked Example with `lapply`

```r
numbers <- list(a = 1:5, b = 10:15, c = c(2, 4, 6, 8))
sums <- lapply(numbers, sum)
# Returns: list(a = 15, b = 75, c = 20)
```

### Worked Example with `sapply`

```r
sums_simplified <- sapply(numbers, sum)
# Returns: named numeric vector: a 15, b 75, c 20
```

> [!NOTE]
> The only difference between `lapply` and `sapply` is the **return simplification step**. `sapply` internally calls `simplify2array()` on the `lapply` output.

## 3.3 Memory Representation

Lists in R are stored as a **pairlist of SEXP pointers**. Each component is a pointer to an S-expression (the actual object), which means:

- **Copy-on-modify semantics**: Modifying a list creates a new binding rather than mutating in place.
- **Memory efficiency**: A list of large objects only stores references (pointers), not copies.
- **Lazy evaluation**: Function arguments stored in a list are not evaluated until accessed (relevant for non-standard evaluation).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Memory Layout of an R List

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how a list is stored as a linked structure of pointers (SEXPs) to actual R objects.

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1["List iot_sensor"]:::listNode
    L1 --> P1["Pointer to chr"]:::ptr
    L1 --> P2["Pointer to chr"]:::ptr
    L1 --> P3["Pointer to num vector"]:::ptr
    L1 --> P4["Pointer to lgl"]:::ptr
    L1 --> P5["Pointer to list"]:::ptr
    P5 --> P5a["Pointer to num offset"]:::ptr
    P5 --> P5b["Pointer to num scale"]:::ptr
    P5 --> P5c["Pointer to chr date"]:::ptr

    classDef listNode fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ptr fill:#E0F2FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Indexing Mechanism

This diagram shows how the three indexing operators (`[ ]`, `[[ ]]`, `$`) interact with a list to produce different outputs.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Input List L with 3 components"]:::inputNode
    A --> B["Operator Selection"]:::processNode
    B --> B1["Use L square bracket i"]:::procBranch
    B --> B2["Use L double bracket i"]:::procBranch
    B --> B3["Use L dollar name"]:::procBranch
    B1 --> C1["Output: Sub-list of length 1, type list"]:::outputNode
    B2 --> C2["Output: Extracted atomic object, original type"]:::outputNode
    B3 --> C3["Output: Extracted atomic object by name"]:::outputNode

    C1 --> D1["Use when you want to SUBSET"]:::note
    C2 --> D2["Use when you want to EXTRACT for computation"]:::note
    C3 --> D3["Use when name is known, readability"]:::note

    classDef inputNode fill:#FFD1DC,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef processNode fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef procBranch fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#9370DB,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

## 4.3 Recursive Nesting Tree

```mermaid

    R["Root List employee"]:::root

    R --> E2["skills: chr vector of length 3"]:::leaf

    E3 --> E3b["pin: num 682001"]:::leaf
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Tree**: To access `project2$hours`, you would write: `employee$projects[[2]]$hours`. Each `[[ ]]` dives **one level deeper** into the list hierarchy.

## 4.4 Operation Flow: Creating → Manipulating → Extracting

```mermaid

    S2["Step 2: Inspect using str and names"]:::step

    S5["Step 5: Extract final results for analysis"]:::step

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5

```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a list in R. How is it different from a vector?

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **list** in R is a non-atomic, recursive data structure that can contain elements of **different data types and lengths**, including vectors, matrices, functions, and other lists. It is created using the `list()` function.

**Differences from a vector:**

| Feature | Vector | List |
|---|---|---|
| Data types | Homogeneous (single type) | Heterogeneous (mixed types allowed) |
| Structure | Atomic | Recursive |
| Creation | `c()` | `list()` |
| Nesting | Cannot contain vectors of different lengths | Can contain lists within lists |

Lists are essential for storing complex, mixed-type data such as the output of statistical models or hierarchical records. *(3 marks)*

### Question 2: Explain the difference between `L[1]` and `L[[1]]` with an example.

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Consider `L <- list(10, 20, 30)`.

- `L[1]` returns a **list containing the first element**: `list(10)`. The result is still of type `list`, useful for subsetting.
- `L[[1]]` returns the **actual first element**: `10` (an atomic numeric). The result is of type `numeric`, useful for computation.

Example:
```r
class(L[1])    # Output: "list"
class(L[[1]])  # Output: "numeric"
```

Using `L[1] + 5` would cause an error (non-numeric), while `L[[1]] + 5` correctly returns `15`. *(3 marks)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style — Module 1 Choice]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the various ways to create, access, and modify lists in R with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

**Creation (3 marks):**

Lists are created using the `list()` constructor. Each argument becomes a component. Components can optionally be named.

```r
# Unnamed list
L1 <- list(1, "a", TRUE)

# Named list
L2 <- list(id = 101, name = "Karthik", scores = c(78, 85, 92))
```

The `names()` function retrieves or assigns names.

**Access (2 marks):**

Three operators are used:
1. `L[1]` → Returns a sub-list (type preserved).
2. `L[[1]]` → Returns the actual element.
3. `L$name` → Returns element by name.

```r
L2[["scores"]]     # Returns numeric vector
L2$scores          # Same as above
L2[1]              # Returns list(id = 101)
```

**Modification (2 marks):**

```r
L2$age <- 22          # Add new component
L2$id <- NULL         # Remove component
L2[["scores"]] <- c(80, 88, 95)  # Update component
```

Lists can be concatenated with `c(L1, L2)` and flattened with `unlist()`.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Write an R program to:**
1. Create a nested list representing a **library catalog** with two books, each containing title, author, year, and an inner list of borrowers.
2. Compute the total number of books borrowed across both books using `lapply` or `sapply`.

**Model Solution:**

```r
library_catalog <- list(
  book1 = list(
    title    = "Introduction to R",
    author   = "Wickham",
    year     = 2014,
    borrowers = list("Anil", "Beena", "Cyril")
  ),
  book2 = list(
    title    = "Data Science with R",
    author   = "Lander",
    year     = 2017,
    borrowers = list("Deepa", "Eshan")
  )
)

# Compute lengths using lapply
borrow_counts <- lapply(library_catalog, function(x) length(x$borrowers))
print(borrow_counts)
# $book1
# [1] 3
# $book2
# [1] 2

# Total using unlist + sum
total_borrowed <- sum(unlist(borrow_counts))
print(total_borrowed)
# [1] 5
```

**Explanation:**
- A nested list is constructed with two books, each containing an inner list of borrowers.
- `lapply()` iterates over each book, applying a custom function that counts borrowers.
- `unlist()` flattens the result to a numeric vector, and `sum()` computes the total.
- **Output**: Total books borrowed = **5**. *(7 marks)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - June 2023 Style — Module 1 Choice]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the functions `lapply()`, `sapply()`, and `mapply()` in R with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

**lapply() (2 marks):**

`lapply(X, FUN, ...)` applies function `FUN` to each element of list/vector `X` and **always returns a list**.

```r
nums <- list(a = 1:3, b = 4:6)
lapply(nums, mean)
# $a  [1] 2
# $b  [1] 5
```

**sapply() (2 marks):**

`sapply(X, FUN, ...)` is a **user-friendly wrapper** around `lapply()` that attempts to **simplify** the result to a vector or matrix.

```r
sapply(nums, mean)
# a  b
# 2  5
```

**mapply() (2 marks):**

`mapply(FUN, ..., MoreArgs, SIMPLIFY)` applies `FUN` to **multiple lists/vectors in parallel**. It is the multivariate version of `sapply`.

```r
mapply(sum, 1:3, 4:6)
# [1] 5 7 9   (i.e., 1+4, 2+5, 3+6)
```

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Function | Output Type | Multi-input | Simplification |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lapply` | List | No (single input) | Never |
| `sapply` | Simplified | No | Automatic |
| `mapply` | Simplified | Yes | Optional via `SIMPLIFY` |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Given a list of student records containing marks in 3 subjects, write an R program using `sapply` to:**
1. Calculate the total marks for each student.
2. Identify students who scored more than 200 marks.
3. Compute the class average total.

**Model Solution:**

```r
students <- list(
  Anil  = c(85, 90, 78),
  Beena = c(72, 65, 80),
  Cyril = c(95, 88, 92),
  Deepa = c(60, 70, 65)
)

# 1. Total marks per student
totals <- sapply(students, sum)
print(totals)
# Anil  Beena  Cyril  Deepa
# 253    217    275    195

# 2. Students with total > 200
toppers <- names(totals[totals > 200])
print(toppers)
# [1] "Anil"  "Beena" "Cyril"

# 3. Class average total
class_avg <- mean(totals)
print(class_avg)
# [1] 235
```

**Explanation:**
- `sapply(students, sum)` applies `sum()` to each student's vector of marks and returns a **named numeric vector**.
- Boolean subsetting `totals > 200` creates a logical vector, which is used to filter names.
- `mean(totals)` computes the class average = `(253 + 217 + 275 + 195) / 4 = 235`. *(7 marks)*

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 3 Marks-Losing Mistakes in Lists Questions:**
> 1. **Using `[ ]` instead of `[[ ]]` for computation** → Causes `non-numeric argument to binary operator` errors or incorrect types. (-2 to -3 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting that `lapply()` always returns a list** → Students often write `result[1]` expecting a vector and lose marks for not knowing the return type. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Not showing the `str()` output or not naming components** → Examiners award marks for clarity; unnamed lists lose 0.5–1 mark per sub-part for not demonstrating best practices.
> 4. **Confusing `$` with `[[ ]]`** when names contain spaces or special characters → Use `[[ ]]` with backticks for such cases. (-1 mark)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Lists in R**

- ⭐ A **list** is the most flexible R data structure; it is **heterogeneous** and **recursive**.
- ⭐ Created with `list()`; named components are best practice.
- ⭐ **Three indexing operators**:
  - `[ ]` → Subset (returns list)
  - `[[ ]]` → Extract (returns the actual object)
  - `$` → Extract by name (syntactic sugar for `[[ ]]`)
- ⭐ `length(L)` counts top-level components only.
- ⭐ `str(L)` reveals the complete structure — always use it during debugging.
- ⭐ `lapply()` always returns a **list**; `sapply()` attempts simplification.
- ⭐ `mapply()` applies a function over **multiple** lists in parallel.
- ⭐ `unlist()` flattens a list into a vector (with type coercion).
- ⭐ `c(L1, L2)` concatenates; `NULL` assignment removes a component.
- ⭐ Lists are the **internal representation** of data frames and most model objects in R.
- ⭐ Lists use **1-based indexing** (R convention).
- ⭐ The `$` operator performs **partial matching** — avoid relying on it; use explicit `[[ ]]` for safety.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory anchor**: *"Lists are R's Swiss Army knife — heterogeneous, recursive, and the foundation of data frames."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors and Tables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET862 - PROGRAMMING AND DATA ANALYTICS WITH R
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Factors and Tables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Factors in R

### Formal Definition
A **factor** in R is a data structure used to represent **categorical (qualitative) data**. Internally, R stores factors as integer vectors with a corresponding set of character *levels* (distinct categories). The `factor()` function converts a vector of character or integer values into a factor object.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Factors are specifically designed to handle nominal (unordered) and ordinal (ordered) categorical variables. They are memory-efficient because R stores the data as integers internally while displaying the level labels.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **factor** as a **color-coded filing system** in an office:
- Imagine you have a stack of 1,000 customer feedback forms.
- Instead of writing "Satisfied", "Neutral", "Dissatisfied" repeatedly (which takes space), you assign each feedback a tiny integer code: 1 = Satisfied, 2 = Neutral, 3 = Dissatisfied.
- The **integer codes** are the underlying storage, and the **lookup dictionary** (the "levels" attribute) tells you what each code means.
- This is exactly how R factors work: they save memory and enable proper statistical treatment of categorical data.

### Why Factors Matter
1. **Statistical Modeling**: R's `lm()`, `glm()`, and `aov()` functions automatically treat factors as categorical predictors, generating dummy variables.
2. **Plotting**: `ggplot2` and base R plotting functions use factor levels to control axis ordering, color grouping, and legends.
3. **Data Validation**: Factors restrict values to a predefined set, catching typos and unexpected categories.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Key Variants of Factors:**
> - **Unordered Factor (Nominal)**: Categories have no natural order. Example: `c("Male", "Female", "Other")`. Created with `factor()`.
> - **Ordered Factor (Ordinal)**: Categories have a meaningful sequence. Example: `c("Low", "Medium", "High")`. Created with `factor(..., ordered = TRUE)`.

---

## 1.2 Tables in R

### Formal Definition
A **table** in R is an object of class `"table"` that stores the **frequency counts** of categorical data, typically resulting from cross-tabulating one or more factors. The `table()` function tallies how many times each combination of factor levels occurs.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **table** as a **census tally sheet**:
- Imagine a teacher with a class roster and a grade book.
- They want to know: "How many students got 'A' in Section A?" "How many got 'B' in Section B?"
- They create a grid (rows = grades, columns = sections) and count entries in each cell.
- This grid is exactly a contingency table in R, and the `table()` function is the teacher's counting tool.

### Types of Tables in R

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Mandated Table Functions:**
> | Function | Purpose | Returns |
> |---|---|---|
> | `table()` | Frequency count of factor levels | 1D, 2D, or nD array |
> | `xtabs()` | Cross-tabulation using formula syntax | Contingency table |
> | `ftable()` | "Flat" display of multi-dimensional tables | Compact 2D display |
> | `prop.table()` | Convert counts to proportions | Same shape as input table |
> | `margin.table()` | Sum across rows/columns of a table | Marginal totals |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Factor

A factor object in R has three internal components:

1. **Integer codes** — the actual data stored as `integer` type.
2. **`levels` attribute** — a character vector giving the unique category names.
3. **`class` attribute** — either `"factor"` (nominal) or `c("ordered", "factor")` (ordinal).

### Logical Workflow of Factor Creation

- **Step 1 — Input Vector**: You start with a character or numeric vector, e.g., `c("Red", "Blue", "Red", "Green")`.
- **Step 2 — Level Detection**: R scans the vector and identifies the unique values, then sorts them **alphabetically by default**.
- **Step 3 — Integer Mapping**: Each unique value is assigned an integer code (1, 2, 3, ...).
- **Step 4 — Attribute Attachment**: The `levels` attribute is attached, and the class is set to `"factor"`.
- **Step 5 — Display**: When printed, R looks up the integer codes in the levels vector and shows the original category names.

> [!NOTE]
> **How (`Why` and `How`):** Factors are memory-efficient because storing 1 million entries as `"Satisfied"` (9 characters × 1 million = ~9 MB) is wasteful. Storing them as integers 1, 2, 3 (4 bytes × 1 million = ~4 MB) plus one small levels vector is dramatically cheaper.

---

## 2.2 Factor-Related Functions

| Function | Syntax | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `factor()` | `factor(x, levels, labels, ordered)` | Create a factor |
| `levels()` | `levels(f)` | Get or set the levels |
| `nlevels()` | `nlevels(f)` | Count the number of levels |
| `relevel()` | `relevel(f, ref)` | Reorder reference level (useful in modeling) |
| `droplevels()` | `droplevels(f)` | Remove unused levels |
| `cut()` | `cut(x, breaks, labels)` | Bin numeric data into a factor |
| `as.factor()` | `as.factor(x)` | Coerce an object to factor |
| `is.factor()` | `is.factor(x)` | Test if object is a factor |

---

## 2.3 Table-Related Functions

| Function | Syntax | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `table()` | `table(... , useNA)` | Frequency count |
| `xtabs()` | `xtabs(formula, data)` | Cross-tab using formula |
| `ftable()` | `ftable(x, row.vars, col.vars)` | Flat contingency table |
| `prop.table()` | `prop.table(x, margin)` | Proportions (row/column/overall) |
| `margin.table()` | `margin.table(x, margin)` | Marginal sums |
| `addmargins()` | `addmargins(x, margin)` | Add marginal totals |
| `CrossTable()` | `CrossTable(x, y)` | Pretty-printed 2D table (gmodels pkg) |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequency and Proportion Formulas Used in Contingency Tables**

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Cell Frequency | $n_{ij}$ | Count in row $i$, column $j$ |
| Row Total | $n_{i\cdot} = \sum_{j=1}^{c} n_{ij}$ | Sum across columns in row $i$ |
| Column Total | $n_{\cdot j} = \sum_{i=1}^{r} n_{ij}$ | Sum across rows in column $j$ |
| Grand Total | $N = \sum_{i=1}^{r}\sum_{j=1}^{c} n_{ij}$ | Total of all cells |
| Row Proportion | $p_{ij}^{(r)} = \dfrac{n_{ij}}{n_{i\cdot}}$ | Proportion within a row |
| Column Proportion | $p_{ij}^{(c)} = \dfrac{n_{ij}}{n_{\cdot j}}$ | Proportion within a column |
| Overall Proportion | $p_{ij} = \dfrac{n_{ij}}{N}$ | Cell's share of grand total |
| Chi-Square Statistic | $\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{r}\sum_{j=1}^{c} \dfrac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$ | Test of independence |
| Expected Frequency | $E_{ij} = \dfrac{n_{i\cdot} \cdot n_{\cdot j}}{N}$ | Expected count under $H_0$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Rule:** In every formula above, the absolute value and division use LaTeX-safe symbols. When writing these formulas inside the markdown table, no `\|` is used — only `\vert` or standard fraction notation.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Quality Control**: Engineers use factor tables to tabulate defect types against production shifts.
- **Survey Analysis**: Categorical survey responses (e.g., "Strongly Agree" to "Strongly Disagree") are stored as ordered factors and cross-tabulated.
- **Machine Learning Preprocessing**: Categorical features are encoded as factors before being passed to decision trees, random forests, or regression models.
- **A/B Testing**: Conversion rates across experimental groups are compared using `prop.table()` outputs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Creating and Inspecting Factors — Worked Example

### Step 1: Create a Character Vector

```r
gender <- c("Male", "Female", "Female", "Male", "Male", "Female", "Other")
```

Here, we store seven entries as plain characters. At this stage, R treats `gender` as a character vector — no concept of "valid categories" exists.

### Step 2: Convert to a Factor

```r
gender_factor <- factor(gender)
print(gender_factor)
```

**Output:**
```
[1] Male   Female Female Male   Male   Female Other
Levels: Female Male Other
```

**Logic:** R scans the unique values, sorts them alphabetically (`Female`, `Male`, `Other`), assigns integer codes `2, 3, 1, 3, 3, 2, 1` internally, and attaches them as the `levels` attribute.

### Step 3: Inspect Internal Structure

```r
str(gender_factor)
```

**Output:**
```
Factor w/ 3 levels "Female","Male","Other": 2 3 3 2 2 3 1
```

**Logic:** The internal integer codes are revealed — proving R stores factors as integers with a level lookup table.

### Step 4: Create an Ordered Factor

```r
rating <- factor(c("Low", "High", "Medium", "Low", "High"),
                 levels = c("Low", "Medium", "High"),
                 ordered = TRUE)
print(rating)
```

**Output:**
```
[1] Low    High   Medium Low    High
Levels: Low < Medium < High
```

**Logic:** The `ordered = TRUE` flag creates an ordinal factor. The `<` symbols between levels indicate a meaningful ordering. This is critical for functions that respect order (e.g., `median()` on ordered factors).

### Step 5: Explicitly Set Levels and Labels

```r
sizes <- factor(c("S", "L", "M", "XL", "S", "L"),
                levels = c("S", "M", "L", "XL"),
                labels = c("Small", "Medium", "Large", "Extra Large"))
print(sizes)
```

**Output:**
```
[1] Small       Large       Medium      Extra Large Small       Large
Levels: Small Medium Large Extra Large
```

**Logic:** The `levels` argument controls the *order* and *valid set* of categories; the `labels` argument provides display names. Any input value not matching `levels` becomes `NA`.

### Step 6: Relevel a Factor

```r
gender_factor <- relevel(gender_factor, ref = "Male")
levels(gender_factor)
```

**Output:**
```
[1] "Male"   "Female" "Other"
```

**Logic:** `relevel()` moves a specified level to the first position, making it the reference category in regression models. This is essential for proper dummy variable encoding.

### Step 7: Drop Unused Levels

```r
small_factor <- factor(c("A", "B", "A", "A", "B"))
levels(small_factor)  # Returns "A" "B"

# Subset to keep only "A"
small_subset <- small_factor[small_factor == "A"]
levels(small_subset)  # Still returns "A" "B" — wasteful!

small_clean <- droplevels(small_subset)
levels(small_clean)   # Returns "A" — clean!
```

**Logic:** Subsetting a factor does **not** automatically remove unused levels. `droplevels()` explicitly prunes them, saving memory and avoiding errors in functions like `tapply()` or `aggregate()`.

---

## 3.2 Building Tables — Worked Example

### Step 1: Create Two Factors

```r
treatment <- factor(c("Drug", "Placebo", "Drug", "Drug", "Placebo", "Placebo", "Drug"))
outcome   <- factor(c("Improved", "No Change", "Improved", "Cured", "No Change", "Improved", "Cured"))
```

### Step 2: One-Way Frequency Table

```r
table(treatment)
```

**Output:**
```
treatment
 Drug Placebo 
    4       3 
```

**Logic:** `table()` counts occurrences of each level. Result is a 1D named integer vector (technically an array of class `"table"`).

### Step 3: Two-Way Contingency Table

```r
tab <- table(treatment, outcome)
print(tab)
```

**Output:**
```
         outcome
treatment Cured Improved No Change
   Drug      1        2         0
   Placebo   0        1         2
```

**Logic:** Each cell $n_{ij}$ counts the number of observations where `treatment = i` and `outcome = j`.

### Step 4: Add Marginal Totals

```r
addmargins(tab)
```

**Output:**
```
         outcome
treatment Cured Improved No Change Sum
   Drug      1        2         0   3
   Placebo   0        1         2   3
   Sum       1        3         2   6
```

**Logic:** `addmargins()` appends row sums (rightmost column) and column sums (bottom row), and the grand total (bottom-right).

### Step 5: Proportion Tables

**Row proportions** — what fraction of each treatment group had each outcome?

```r
prop.table(tab, margin = 1)
```

**Output (computed step by step):**

The row total for `Drug` is $n_{1\cdot} = 1 + 2 + 0 = 3$. The row total for `Placebo` is $n_{2\cdot} = 0 + 1 + 2 = 3$. The grand total is $N = 6$.

$$
p_{11}^{(r)} = \frac{n_{11}}{n_{1\cdot}} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333
$$

$$
p_{12}^{(r)} = \frac{n_{12}}{n_{1\cdot}} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667
$$

$$
p_{13}^{(r)} = \frac{n_{13}}{n_{1\cdot}} = \frac{0}{3} = 0
$$

$$
p_{21}^{(r)} = \frac{n_{21}}{n_{2\cdot}} = \frac{0}{3} = 0
$$

$$
p_{22}^{(r)} = \frac{n_{22}}{n_{2\cdot}} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333
$$

$$
p_{23}^{(r)} = \frac{n_{23}}{n_{2\cdot}} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667
$$

Resulting R output:
```
         outcome
treatment    Cured   Improved   No Change
   Drug     0.3333     0.6667      0.0000
   Placebo  0.0000     0.3333      0.6667
```

**Column proportions** — within each outcome, what fraction came from each treatment?

```r
prop.table(tab, margin = 2)
```

Column total for `Cured`: $n_{\cdot 1} = 1 + 0 = 1$. So $p_{11}^{(c)} = 1/1 = 1$ and $p_{21}^{(c)} = 0/1 = 0$.

**Overall proportions:**

```r
prop.table(tab)
```

For example, $p_{11} = 1/6 \approx 0.167$.

### Step 6: xtabs() — Formula-Based Cross-Tabulation

```r
df <- data.frame(treatment, outcome)
xtabs(~ treatment + outcome, data = df)
```

**Output:**
```
         outcome
treatment Cured Improved No Change
   Drug      1        2         0
   Placebo   0        1         2
```

**Logic:** `xtabs()` uses R's formula interface `~ row_var + col_var`. This is the preferred syntax when working with data frames and is the gateway to fitting log-linear models (`loglin()`).

### Step 7: ftable() — Flat Display for 3D Tables

```r
# Add a third factor
gender <- factor(c("M", "F", "M", "F", "F", "M", "F"))
tab3d <- table(treatment, outcome, gender)
print(tab3d)  # Messy 3D array

ftable(tab3d)
```

**Output:**
```
            gender F M
treatment outcome       
Drug       Cured    0 1
           Improved 1 1
           No Change 0 0
Placebo    Cured    0 0
           Improved 1 0
           No Change 2 0
```

**Logic:** `ftable()` collapses higher-dimensional tables into a compact 2D "flat" view, which is far easier to read.

### Step 8: Margin Tables

```r
margin.table(tab, margin = 1)  # Row sums
margin.table(tab, margin = 2)  # Column sums
```

**Logic:** `margin.table(x, margin)` sums over all dimensions **except** the one specified. Useful for isolating row totals or column totals for further analysis.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Operation | Input | Function | Output Shape | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Create factor | Vector | `factor()` | Factor vector | Categorical data |
| Bin numeric | Numeric | `cut()` | Factor | Discretization |
| Count | 1+ factors | `table()` | Table object | Frequency analysis |
| Cross-tab | Data frame | `xtabs()` | Table object | Modeling input |
| Flatten 3D+ | Table | `ftable()` | 2D table | Display |
| Normalize | Table | `prop.table()` | Same shape | Proportion analysis |
| Sum margins | Table | `margin.table()` | Vector | Marginal analysis |
| Chi-square | 2D table | `chisq.test()` | htest object | Independence test |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
