The function must *return* a string, not call `stop()`. The whole point is that `setClass` collects the returned string and raises a uniform error with it. Using `stop()` directly bypasses the validity protocol and is marked down **1 mark**.

---

### Question B (14 marks) — *Generics, Methods, and Multiple Dispatch*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** What is a *generic function* in S4? Write R code to:
> 1. Define a generic `describe` that takes one argument.
> 2. Define a method for class `Employee` that prints `"Employee with id=<id> earns <salary>"`.
> 3. Define a method for class `Manager` that prints `"Manager leads team of <teamSize>"`.

> **(b) [7 marks]** Demonstrate **multiple dispatch** by defining a generic `compareSalary` that takes two arguments (both of class `Employee` or its subclass) and returns the difference in salary. Show the code and the output when comparing a regular `Employee` with a `Manager`.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

*Definition (2 marks):* A **generic function** in S4 is a *placeholder function* that does not perform any computation itself; instead, it *dispatches* to a method whose **signature** matches the class(es) of the input argument(s). It is registered with `setGeneric()` and its body must call `standardGeneric()`.

```r
setGeneric("describe", function(x) standardGeneric("describe"))   # 1 mark

setMethod("describe", "Employee", function(x) {                  # 1.5 marks
  cat("Employee with id=", x@id,
      " earns ", x@salary, "\n", sep = "")
})

setMethod("describe", "Manager", function(x) {                   # 1.5 marks
  cat("Manager leads team of ", x@teamSize, "\n", sep = "")
})

# Demonstration [1 mark]
describe(e1)        # "Employee with id=E101 earns 50000"
describe(manager1)  # "Manager leads team of 10"
```

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

```r
setGeneric("compareSalary",
  function(x, y) standardGeneric("compareSalary")               # 1 mark
)

setMethod("compareSalary",                                       # 3 marks
  signature(x = "Employee", y = "Employee"),
  function(x, y) {
    diff <- x@salary - y@salary
    cat("Salary difference: ", diff, "\n", sep = "")
    invisible(diff)
  }
)
```

*Multiple-dispatch demonstration [3 marks]:*

```r
e1   <- new("Employee", id = "E101", salary = 50000, dept = "IT")
mgr1 <- new("Manager",  id = "M001", salary = 80000, teamSize = 10)

compareSalary(mgr1, e1)   # Salary difference: 30000
```

*Why this is "multiple dispatch":* The generic has **two arguments** (`x`, `y`). When `compareSalary(mgr1, e1)` is called, R looks at the *class* of **both** arguments (`"Manager"` and `"Employee"`), finds the **most specific** signature that covers this pair (here, the `(Employee, Employee)` signature works because `Manager` *is-a* `Employee`), and dispatches accordingly.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** Students often write a *single* method with a manual `if (is(x,"Manager"))` check. This is **not multiple dispatch** — it is *manual polymorphism*. Multiple dispatch **must** use the `setMethod(..., signature=...)` mechanism. Manual `if`-cascades lose **2 full marks** under the KTU 2024 marking scheme.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ S4 = *formal*, *typed*, *generic-based* OOP system in R; lives in the `methods` package.
- ✅ Use `setClass("Name", slots=c(a="numeric", b="character"))` to **define**.
- ✅ Use `new("Name", a=1, b="hi")` to **instantiate**.
- ✅ Use `obj@slot` or `slot(obj, "slot")` to **read**; `slot(obj,"slot") <- val` to **write**.
- ✅ Use `contains = "Parent"` for **inheritance**; the child inherits all parent slots.
- ✅ Use `validity = function(object){ ... }` for **automatic** sanity checks; return `TRUE` or a *character vector* of errors.
- ✅ Use `setGeneric("f", function(...) standardGeneric("f"))` to **register a generic**.
- ✅ Use `setMethod("f", signature(x="A", y="B"), function(x,y){...})` to **bind behaviour**.
- ✅ S4 supports **multiple dispatch** — look at *all* signature arguments.
- ✅ **Introspection**: `isClass`, `isVirtual`, `validObject`, `slotNames`, `getSlots`, `extends`.
- ✅ **Virtual classes** have no `prototype`; they exist only for inheritance.
- ✅ **Union classes** (`setClassUnion`) allow a slot to hold one of several types.
- ✅ S4 is the *backbone* of Bioconductor — master it for genomics / pharma R roles.
- 🚫 **Never** use `class(x) <-` for S4 (that's S3 syntax).
- 🚫 **Never** access S4 slots with `$` (that's for lists / data.frames).
- 🚫 **Never** use `stop()` inside a `validity` function — *return* a string instead.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: R6 Classes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET862 - PROGRAMMING AND DATA ANALYTICS WITH R
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** R6 Classes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **R6 Class** is an object-oriented programming (OOP) system in R, implemented through the `R6` package, that provides **encapsulated, reference-semantics classes** supporting public and private members, inheritance, active bindings, and finalizers. Unlike R's traditional S3 and S4 systems, R6 objects are **mutable** (modified in place) and behave like objects in Python, Java, or C++.

In R6 terminology:

- **Class**: A blueprint defining the structure (fields) and behaviour (methods) of objects.
- **Field**: A variable that holds data inside an object (object state).
- **Method**: A function bound to a class that operates on the object's fields.
- **Object / Instance**: A concrete realization of a class created using the `clone()` method.
- **Encapsulation**: Restricting direct access to some object components using `public` and `private` lists.
- **Active Binding**: A field-like interface backed by a function — accessed like a variable but computed on demand.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Contract" View

> [!NOTE]
> Think of an **R6 Class** as a **Smart Building Blueprint**.
> - The **blueprint** (the class) defines the rooms (fields) and the operating instructions for each room (methods).
> - Each **constructed building** (the object) is a real instance — independent, addressable, and modifiable.
> - **Private rooms** (private members) can only be accessed through the lobby reception desk (public methods), not directly.
> - **Active bindings** are like digital displays — they look like static numbers (e.g., "floor temperature") but actually compute fresh values from sensors every time you glance at them.
> - Unlike S3/S4 (where modifying an object creates a *new copy* — like photocopying a document each time you edit), **R6 modifies the original building in place**, which is faster and more memory-efficient for large stateful objects.

## 1.3 Why R6 Was Introduced (KTU Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme stresses that R's default OOP (S3 and S4) follows **copy-on-modify semantics**. This means every time you "modify" an object, R copies it. For data analytics with large models (e.g., simulation, iterative ML training), this is catastrophic for memory and speed. **R6 solves this** by introducing **reference semantics** — the same paradigm used in mainstream OOP languages.

## 1.4 Quick Comparison Snapshot

| Feature | S3 / S4 (Base R) | **R6 Class** |
|---|---|---|
| Semantics | Copy-on-modify | **Reference (in-place)** |
| Encapsulation | None | **public / private** |
| Inheritance | Informal | **Formal `inherit =`** |
| Methods | Generic dispatch | **Bound methods (`$method()`)** |
| Package | Built-in | **`R6` (CRAN)** |
| Active Bindings | Not supported | **Supported** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an R6 Class

An R6 class is constructed using the function `R6::R6Class()`. The signature is:

```
R6Class(
   classname       = NULL,
   public          = list(),
   private         = list(),
   active          = list(),
   inherit         = NULL,
   lock_objects    = FALSE,
   lock_class      = FALSE,
   portable       = TRUE,
   finalize       = NULL
)
```

### 2.1.1 The Three Visibility Lists

> [!NOTE]
> **Public list** — members (fields and methods) accessible from outside via the `$` operator. This is the default and most-used list.
>
> **Private list** — internal members accessible *only* from within other methods of the same class. Accessed via `private$<member>`. Used for true encapsulation.
>
> **Active list** — fields that look like variables but execute a function when read or written. Useful for computed properties, validation, or reactive displays.

### 2.1.2 The `self` and `private` Pointers

Inside any R6 method:

- **`self`** refers to the **current object** (analogue of `this` in Java/C++ or `self` in Python).
- **`private`** refers to the object's private environment.

These two are automatically bound when a method is called.

## 2.2 The Reference-Semantics Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (R6 Reference Semantics)**: For any two variables `a` and `b` bound to the same R6 object, modifications via `a` are visible via `b`, because both refer to the same underlying memory location.

Formally, let `O` be an R6 object. If `a <- O` and `b <- O`, then:

$$\text{id}(a) \equiv \text{id}(b) \equiv \text{id}(O)$$

where `id()` denotes R's internal memory address. This is fundamentally different from base R semantics where `b <- a` would create a copy.

## 2.3 Inheritance

R6 supports single inheritance. A child class inherits all public and private members from the parent, and can override methods or add new ones.

$$\text{ChildClass} = \text{ParentClass} \cup \{\text{new members}\} \cup \{\text{overrides}\}$$

The `inherit = ParentClassObject` argument in `R6Class()` activates this. Inside child methods, the parent implementation can be invoked via `super$method_name()`.

## 2.4 Cloning (Deep Copy on Demand)

Because R6 objects are reference-based, to get a truly independent copy you must call:

$$\text{new\_object} = \text{old\_object}\$ \texttt{clone(deep = TRUE)}$$

The `deep = TRUE` argument ensures that any nested R6 objects within the parent are also cloned (not just the top-level reference).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax / Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Define class | `MyClass <- R6Class("MyClass", public = list(...))` | Class name as first argument |
| Create object | `obj <- MyClass$new()` | Calls `$new()` method |
| Access field | `obj$field_name` | Public only |
| Call method | `obj$method_name(args)` | Method invocation |
| Inside method | `self$other_method()` | Refers to current object |
| Private access | `private$secret_value` | Only inside class methods |
| Active binding | `active = list(x = function(val) {...})` | 1-arg function (read+write) |
| Inheritance | `inherit = ParentClass` | In `R6Class()` call |
| Parent call | `super$method()` | Inside overridden method |
| Clone | `obj2 <- obj$clone(deep = TRUE)` | Independent copy |
| Lock object | `lock_objects = TRUE` | Prevents adding new fields |
| Finalizer | `finalize = function() {...}` | Runs on garbage collection |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **R6 in Industry**: R6 classes are heavily used in production-grade R packages such as `plumber` (REST APIs), `shiny` modules, `torch` (deep learning), `mlr3` (machine learning), and `data.table` internals. For KTU data analytics projects, R6 enables building **reusable, stateful analytics pipelines** — e.g., a `DataCleaner` object that maintains its own cleaning state across operations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Code Execution Mandate**: Every R code block below is fully runnable. Students must run each example in RStudio to consolidate understanding — KTU lab examinations frequently include hands-on R6 tasks.

## 3.1 Example 1 — Creating a Basic R6 Class (Bank Account)

**Problem**: Build a class `BankAccount` that tracks balance and supports deposit, withdrawal, and a balance inquiry.

### Step-by-step construction:

**Step 1** — Install and load the R6 package.

```r
install.packages("R6")   # Run once
library(R6)
```

**Step 2** — Define the class with public fields and methods.

```r
BankAccount <- R6Class(
  classname = "BankAccount",
  public = list(
    # ----- FIELDS -----
    owner   = NULL,
    balance = 0,
    
    # ----- CONSTRUCTOR-LIKE INITIALIZER -----
    initialize = function(owner, opening_balance = 0) {
      self$owner        <- owner
      self$balance      <- opening_balance
      cat("Account opened for", owner, 
          "with balance INR", opening_balance, "\n")
    },
    
    # ----- METHODS -----
    deposit = function(amount) {
      if (amount <= 0) stop("Deposit must be positive")
      self$balance <- self$balance + amount
      cat("Deposited:", amount, "| New balance:", self$balance, "\n")
    },
    
    withdraw = function(amount) {
      if (amount > self$balance) {
        stop("Insufficient funds")
      }
      self$balance <- self$balance - amount
      cat("Withdrew:", amount, "| New balance:", self$balance, "\n")
    },
    
    get_balance = function() {
      return(self$balance)
    }
  )
)
```

**Step 3** — Create objects and use them.

```r
acct1 <- BankAccount$new("Alice", 5000)
acct1$deposit(2000)
acct1$withdraw(1500)
print(acct1$get_balance())   # 5500
```

**Step 4** — Demonstrate reference semantics (KTU favourite question).

```r
acct2 <- acct1                  # Both point to SAME object
acct2$deposit(500)              # Modifies acct1 also!
print(acct1$get_balance())      # 6000  (NOT 5500)
print(acct2$get_balance())      # 6000
```

**Step 5** — Clone to get independent copy.

```r
acct3 <- acct1$clone(deep = TRUE)
acct3$deposit(10000)
print(acct1$get_balance())      # 6000   (unchanged)
print(acct3$get_balance())      # 16000  (independent)
```

## 3.2 Example 2 — Private Members (True Encapsulation)

**Problem**: A class `SecureCounter` with a hidden internal counter that can only be modified via `increment()`.

```r
SecureCounter <- R6Class(
  classname = "SecureCounter",
  
  public = list(
    initialize = function(start = 0) {
      private$count <- start    # accessible via private$count
    },
    
    increment = function(by = 1) {
      if (!is.numeric(by) || by < 0) stop("Must be non-negative number")
      private$count <- private$count + by
    },
    
    show = function() {
      cat("Current count:", private$count, "\n")
    }
  ),
  
  private = list(
    count = 0     # hidden from outside
  )
)

sc <- SecureCounter$new()
sc$increment(5)
sc$show()                  # "Current count: 5"

# sc$count                  # ERROR — cannot access private from outside
```

**Logical derivation of the access check**:

R6 internally maintains two environments per object: one for `public` and one for `private`. When `sc$count` is called, R looks up `count` in the public environment; it is not found, so an error like `'$' operator is invalid for atomic vectors` is raised.

## 3.3 Example 3 — Active Bindings (Computed Properties)

**Problem**: A `Rectangle` class with `width`, `height`, and a computed `area` that always reflects current values.

```r
Rectangle <- R6Class(
  classname = "Rectangle",
  
  public = list(
    width  = NULL,
    height = NULL,
    
    initialize = function(w, h) {
      self$width  <- w
      self$height <- h
    }
  ),
  
  active = list(
    area = function(value) {
      if (missing(value)) {
        # GETTER — called when obj$area is read
        return(self$width * self$height)
      } else {
        # SETTER — called when obj$area <- value is written
        # Solve: width = area / height (assuming height known)
        self$width <- value / self$height
      }
    }
  )
)

r <- Rectangle$new(4, 5)
print(r$area)         # 20  (computed on the fly)
r$width <- 10
print(r$area)         # 50  (re-computed)
r$area <- 100         # uses setter; width becomes 100/5 = 20
print(r$width)        # 20
```

**Derivation of the setter logic**:

We are given the equation of the rectangle's area:

$$A = w \times h$$

To solve for the width when the area is assigned by the user:

$$w = \frac{A}{h} \quad \text{provided} \quad h \neq 0$$

Hence the active binding setter assigns `self$width <- value / self$height`, which is exactly the algebraic rearrangement shown above.

## 3.4 Example 4 — Inheritance and Method Overriding

**Problem**: A base `Animal` class with a `speak()` method, and a `Dog` subclass that overrides it.

```r
Animal <- R6Class(
  classname = "Animal",
  public = list(
    name = NULL,
    initialize = function(name) { self$name <- name },
    speak = function() { cat(self$name, "makes a sound.\n") }
  )
)

Dog <- R6Class(
  classname = "Dog",
  inherit = Animal,
  public = list(
    speak = function() {
      cat(self$name, "says: Woof!\n")
      super$speak()      # optionally invoke parent
    }
  )
)

d <- Dog$new("Bruno")
d$speak()
# Output:
# Bruno says: Woof!
# Bruno makes a sound.
```

**Inheritance derivation**:

$$\text{Dog.speak}() = \text{super.speak}() \oplus \text{``Woof'' message}$$

where $\oplus$ denotes composition of behaviours, illustrating the *is-a* relationship: a Dog **is-an** Animal that barks.

## 3.5 Example 5 — Finalizer (Cleanup on Object Destruction)

```r
FileHandler <- R6Class(
  classname = "FileHandler",
  public = list(
    file_path = NULL,
    initialize = function(path) {
      self$file_path <- path
      cat("Opened:", path, "\n")
    },
    close = function() {
      cat("Closed:", self$file_path, "\n")
    }
  ),
  finalize = function() {
    cat("Finalizer: cleaning up", self$file_path, "\n")
  }
)

fh <- FileHandler$new("data.csv")
fh$close()
rm(fh)
gc()    # triggers finalize() — garbage collection
```

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison: S3 vs R6 (Detailed Derivation)

| Behaviour | S3 (base R) | R6 |
|---|---|---|
| `b <- a` | Copy-on-modify (independent) | Reference (shared) |
| Adding field | Possible (loosely) | Controlled via `lock_objects` |
| Method dispatch | `UseMethod()` generic | Direct call `$method()` |
| Performance for large objects | Slow (copies) | Fast (in-place) |

The performance difference arises because in S3, assignment `b <- a` triggers R's copy-on-modify. When `b` is later modified, R duplicates the underlying vector, giving:

$$\text{Memory}_{S3} = 2 \times \text{size}(a)$$

In contrast, R6 uses environments (reference objects):

$$\text{Memory}_{R6} = 1 \times \text{size}(a) \quad \text{(shared until cloned)}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 R6 Class — Internal Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[R6Class call] --> B[classname string]
    A --> C[public list]
    A --> D[private list]
    A --> E[active list]
    A --> F[inherit parent]
    A --> G[finalize function]

    C --> C1[fields]
    C --> C2[methods]

    D --> D1[hidden fields]
    D --> D2[helper functions]

    E --> E1[getter setter functions]

    F --> H[parent public and private]

    G --> I[runs on garbage collection]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Object Instantiation & Method Call Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Code
    participant N as new method
    participant I as initialize method
    participant O as R6 Object Instance
    participant M as Public Method
    participant P as Private Members

    U->>N: MyClass$new(arg1, arg2)
    N->>I: invoke initialize
    I->>O: set self$field1, self$field2
    O-->>U: return instance
    U->>M: instance$method(args)
    M->>O: read self$field
    M->>P: read/write private$data
    M-->>U: return result
```

## 4.3 Inheritance Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Animal Parent Class] --> B[Dog Child Class]
    A --> C[Cat Child Class]
    B --> D[Puppy Grandchild Class]

    AFields[name field]
    AMethods[speak method]
    BFields[breed field]
    BMethods[speak override]
    CMethods[speak override]
    DFields[toy field]
    DMethods[bark method]

    A --- AFields
    A --- AMethods
    B --- BFields
    B --- BMethods
    C --- CMethods
    D --- DFields
    D --- DMethods
```

## 4.4 Reference Semantics vs Copy Semantics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S3[S3 Base R Copy Semantics]
        S3A[a equals obj1] --> S3B[b is independent copy]
        S3B --> S3C[modify b]
        S3C --> S3D[a unchanged]
    end

    subgraph R6S[R6 Reference Semantics]
        R6A[a equals obj1] --> R6B[b shares same reference]
        R6B --> R6C[modify b]
        R6C --> R6D[both a and b reflect change]
    end

    style S3 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333
    style R6S fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#333
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define an R6 class. Explain how it differs from S3 and S4 classes in R.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
An R6 class is an object-oriented construct created via the `R6::R6Class()` function that supports **reference semantics**, **public/private encapsulation**, **inheritance**, and **active bindings**. Unlike S3 and S4 classes which follow **copy-on-modify semantics** and rely on generic function dispatch, R6 objects are **mutable in-place**, methods are bound directly to the object, and access is controlled via the `public`, `private`, and `active` lists. R6 is therefore suitable for stateful, large-scale, or production-grade data analytics workloads.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the purpose of the `self` and `private` keywords inside an R6 method?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
- **`self`** is an automatic reference to the **current object instance**. It allows a method to read or write its own fields (`self$field`) and call other methods (`self$other_method()`).
- **`private`** is an automatic reference to the object's **private environment**. It enables methods to access members declared inside the `private = list(...)` block, enforcing **encapsulation** by hiding internal data from external code.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Write an R6 class `Student` with fields `name`, `roll_no`, and `marks` (a vector). Include methods to add a mark, compute the average, and display the student details. Demonstrate creating an object and calling all methods. `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution**:

```r
library(R6)

Student <- R6Class(
  classname = "Student",
  
  public = list(
    name    = NULL,
    roll_no = NULL,
    marks   = NULL,
    
    initialize = function(name, roll_no, initial_marks = c()) {
      self$name    <- name
      self$roll_no <- roll_no
      self$marks   <- initial_marks
    },
    
    add_mark = function(m) {
      if (!is.numeric(m) || m < 0 || m > 100) {
        stop("Mark must be numeric between 0 and 100")
      }
      self$marks <- c(self$marks, m)
      cat("Added mark:", m, "\n")
    },
    
    average = function() {
      if (length(self$marks) == 0) return(NA)
      return(mean(self$marks))
    },
    
    display = function() {
      cat("--- Student Details ---\n")
      cat("Name   :", self$name, "\n")
      cat("Roll No:", self$roll_no, "\n")
      cat("Marks  :", self$marks, "\n")
      cat("Average:", round(self$average(), 2), "\n")
    }
  )
)

# Demonstration
s1 <- Student$new("Anjali", "R2024CS101")
s1$add_mark(85)
s1$add_mark(92)
s1$add_mark(78)
s1$display()
```

**Mark Distribution**:
- Class definition syntax: 2 marks
- Public fields and methods: 2 marks
- Logic of add/average/display: 2 marks
- Output demonstration: 1 mark

---

#### (b) Explain the concept of **active bindings** in R6. Write an R6 class `Circle` with a public field `radius` and an active binding `area` that always returns the current area. `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution**:

**Concept (3 marks)**: Active bindings are members of the `active` list in `R6Class()`. They are accessed like ordinary fields using `obj$name` but are backed by a function. The function is called automatically on every read (or write), enabling computed or validated properties. A one-argument function handles both get (when argument is missing) and set (when argument is provided).

**Code (4 marks)**:

```r
library(R6)

Circle <- R6Class(
  classname = "Circle",
  
  public = list(
    radius = NULL,
    
    initialize = function(radius) {
      if (radius < 0) stop("Radius cannot be negative")
      self$radius <- radius
    }
  ),
  
  active = list(
    area = function(value) {
      if (missing(value)) {
        # GETTER
        return(pi * self$radius^2)
      } else {
        # SETTER: solve radius from desired area
        # area = pi * r^2  =>  r = sqrt(area / pi)
        self$radius <- sqrt(value / pi)
      }
    }
  )
)

c1 <- Circle$new(5)
print(c1$area)          # 78.5398...
c1$radius <- 10
print(c1$area)          # 314.159...
c1$area <- 314.159      # setter; radius becomes 10
print(c1$radius)        # 10
```

**Derivation of the setter**:
$$A = \pi r^2 \implies r = \sqrt{\frac{A}{\pi}}$$

This algebraic rearrangement is what makes the active binding reversible.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) Compare and contrast R's traditional OOP (S3/S4) with R6 classes. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of reference semantics for data analytics. `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution**:

| Aspect | S3 / S4 | R6 |
|---|---|---|
| Semantics | Copy-on-modify | Reference (in-place) |
| Encapsulation | None | `public` / `private` |
| Inheritance | Informal | Formal `inherit =` |
| Method access | Generic dispatch | Direct via `$` |
| Active bindings | Not supported | Supported |
| Finalizers | Not supported | Supported |
| Memory for large objects | High (copies) | Low (shared) |

**Advantages of R6 (3 marks)**:
1. **Performance** — avoids repeated copying of large models/datasets.
2. **Encapsulation** — `private` members hide implementation details.
3. **Reusability** — inheritance and active bindings enable clean, modular designs.
4. **Statefulness** — natural fit for iterative ML pipelines, simulations, and APIs.

**Disadvantages of R6 (1 mark)**:
1. Reference semantics can cause **unintended side effects** when multiple variables share the same object — explicit `clone()` is needed for independence.
2. Slightly **steeper learning curve** for users accustomed to functional programming style of base R.

**Conclusion (1 mark)**: For stateful, large-scale, or production analytics systems, R6 is preferred; for quick statistical scripting, S3 remains convenient.

---

#### (b) Demonstrate **inheritance** in R6 by creating a parent class `Vehicle` with a `speed` field and a `move()` method, and a child class `Car` that inherits from `Vehicle`, adds a `brand` field, and overrides the `move()` method. Show how `super$move()` is used. `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution**:

```r
library(R6)

# ----- Parent Class -----
Vehicle <- R6Class(
  classname = "Vehicle",
  public = list(
    speed = NULL,
    
    initialize = function(speed) {
      self$speed <- speed
    },
    
    move = function() {
      cat("Vehicle moves at", self$speed, "km/h.\n")
    }
  )
)

# ----- Child Class -----
Car <- R6Class(
  classname = "Car",
  inherit = Vehicle,
  public = list(
    brand = NULL,
    
    initialize = function(speed, brand) {
      super$initialize(speed)   # call parent constructor
      self$brand <- brand
    },
    
    move = function() {
      cat(self$brand, "car")
      super$move()             # delegate to parent
    }
  )
)

# ----- Demonstration -----
my_car <- Car$new(120, "Toyota")
my_car$move()
# Output:
# Toyota car Vehicle moves at 120 km/h.
```

**Explanation (3 marks for logic)**:
- `inherit = Vehicle` makes `Car` a subclass.
- `super$initialize(speed)` invokes the parent constructor so that `self$speed` is properly initialized.
- Overriding `move()` allows customised behaviour while `super$move()` reuses the parent logic.
- This is the classical *is-a* relationship: a Car **is-a** Vehicle.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in R6 Questions**:
>
> 1. **Confusing `clone()` with assignment**: Students often write `b <- a` thinking it creates an independent copy. In R6 it does **NOT** — both refer to the same object. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this.
>
> 2. **Forgetting `library(R6)`**: The `R6Class()` function is not in base R. Forgetting `library(R6)` at the top of the code will cause runtime errors and **0 marks for execution**.
>
> 3. **Trying to access private members from outside**: `obj$private_field` always fails. Use `public` methods that internally reference `private$field`.
>
> 4. **Misusing active bindings**: A two-argument function in `active` is wrong. The function must take **exactly one argument** (used for both read and write). Using zero arguments disables the setter.
>
> 5. **Confusing `super$method()` with `self$method()`**: `super$` invokes the **parent's** version; `self$` invokes the **current class's** version (which may be the overridden one — infinite recursion risk).
>
> 6. **Not specifying `classname`** in `R6Class()`: While not strictly required, omitting it loses 1 mark on print/display quality in KTU evaluation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — R6 Classes**

- **R6** = Reference-semantics OOP system via `R6` package; loaded with `library(R6)`.
- **Class definition** uses `R6Class("Name", public = list(...), private = list(...), active = list(...), inherit = Parent)`.
- **Public members** are accessible via `obj$member`; **private** only via `private$member` inside methods.
- **Active bindings** are functions with one argument — getter if `missing(value)`, setter otherwise.
- **`self`** = current object; **`private`** = private environment; **`super`** = parent class.
- **Reference semantics**: `b <- a` does *not* copy; use `b <- a$clone(deep = TRUE)` for independent copy.
- **Inheritance** is single; supports method overriding with `super$method()` to call parent.
- **Finalizer** runs on garbage collection (`rm(obj); gc()`).
- **`lock_objects = TRUE`** prevents adding new fields after instantiation.
- **Performance**: R6 avoids copy-on-modify overhead → preferred for large stateful analytics objects.
- **Real-world use**: `plumber`, `shiny` modules, `mlr3`, `torch` — all rely on R6.
- **Comparison to remember**: S3/S4 = copy-semantics + generics; R6 = reference-semantics + encapsulated methods.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Input and Output
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET862 - PROGRAMMING AND DATA ANALYTICS WITH R
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Input and Output

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the R programming language, **Input and Output (I/O)** refers to the controlled mechanisms through which data is acquired from external sources (keyboard, files, URLs, databases) into the R workspace, and the structured methods through which processed data, messages, results, and visualizations are exported back to the user console, external files, or graphical devices.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Programming and Data Analytics with R*, the I/O subsystem is formally classified into three primary domains:

1. **Console I/O** — Interaction between the user and the R interpreter via the Read-Eval-Print Loop (REPL).
2. **File I/O** — Persistent storage operations involving flat files (`.txt`, `.csv`, `.dat`), binary files, and compressed formats.
3. **Connection-Based I/O** — Abstract stream handling using R's `connection` object, enabling reads from URLs, pipes, and gzip-compressed archives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The course specifically mandates mastery of the `scan()`, `readline()`, `read.csv()`, `read.table()`, `print()`, `cat()`, `paste()`, `sprintf()`, `writeLines()`, `write.csv()`, and `sink()` functions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine R as a **highly disciplined restaurant kitchen**:

- **Input** is the *delivery bay* where ingredients (data) arrive — either handed directly to the chef (keyboard via `readline()`), or delivered in pre-packaged crates (CSV files via `read.csv()`).
- **Output** is the *serving window* where finished dishes (results) are plated and sent out — either spoken to a waiter (console via `print()`/`cat()`) or packed into takeout containers (files via `write.csv()`).
- The **`sink()`** function acts like a *floor manager* who redirects all outgoing orders to a particular counter for a specific duration.

This analogy helps visualize that R offers multiple "lanes" for both receiving data and dispatching results, and the programmer's job is to pick the right lane for the right data type.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction:** `print()` is an *R-object-aware* display function (preserves quotes, dimensions, class info), while `cat()` is a *raw concatenation* function that outputs literal characters without decoration. This subtle difference is a frequent KTU exam question.

## 1.3 The REPL Architecture

Every R session operates inside a **Read–Eval–Print Loop (REPL)**. When you type an expression:

1. **Read** — The interpreter reads the line.
2. **Eval** — The expression is evaluated in the global environment.
3. **Print** — The result is automatically printed using an implicit call to `print()`.

This loop is why simply typing a variable name at the prompt produces visible output, while calling the same variable inside a function body produces nothing unless explicitly printed.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Encoding

| Constant / Parameter | Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Default working directory** | `getwd()` | Platform-dependent; e.g., `C:/Users/...` |
| **Default line separator** | `\n` | Unix-style newline |
| **Default field separator (CSV)** | `","` | Comma |
| **Default missing value token** | `NA` | Logical constant for missing data |
| **Decimal separator** | `"."` | Locale-dependent, alterable via `options(OutDec=",")` |
| **Encoding default** | `"unknown"` | Replaced by `readr`-style UTF-8 in modern R |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Console Input Functions

### 2.1.1 `readline(prompt = "")`

- **Purpose:** Reads a **single line** of text from the terminal as a character string.
- **Mechanism:** Internally calls `readLines("stdin", n=1)`.
- **Type Coercion:** Returns *character* — numeric input requires explicit `as.numeric()` wrapping.

```r
name <- readline("Enter your name: ")
age  <- as.numeric(readline("Enter your age: "))
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does R return character by default?** Because the terminal accepts *any* keystroke including letters, R cannot pre-classify the input. The burden of type conversion lies on the programmer — a classic KTU viva question.

### 2.1.2 `scan(file = "", what = double(), nmax = -1, sep = "", ...)`

- **Purpose:** A high-throughput, vectorized reader. Unlike `readline()`, `scan()` can ingest **multiple values on one line** or **multiple lines** into a vector.
- **Parameters of Interest:**
  - `what`: Placeholder type (`double()`, `character()`, `integer()`, `logical()`) — declares the output vector mode.
  - `sep`: Field separator; default `""` means whitespace (spaces, tabs, newlines).
  - `nmax`: Maximum number of items to read; `-1` reads all.
  - `skip`: Number of lines to skip at the start (useful for skipping headers).

```r
# Reading 5 numbers from keyboard
nums <- scan(what = double(), nmax = 5)
# Output entered: 10 20 30 40 50
# nums becomes c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50)
```

## 2.2 Console Output Functions

### 2.2.1 `print(x, digits = NULL, quote = TRUE, ...)`

- **Behavior:** R-object dispatcher. The function dispatches based on the class of `x` via `UseMethod("print")`.
- **Key Flag:** `quote = FALSE` strips quotation marks from strings.

### 2.2.2 `cat(..., file = "", sep = " ", fill = FALSE, labels = NULL, append = FALSE)`

- **Purpose:** Concatenates and prints arguments *without* quotes, indices, or class metadata.
- **Critical Difference from `print()`:**

| Feature | `print()` | `cat()` |
|---|---|---|
| Quotes around strings | Yes (default) | No |
| Vector indices `[1]` | Yes | No |
| Newline appended | No (sometimes) | No (caller adds `\n`) |
| Multi-argument | Single object | Variadic `...` |
| Returns | Invisible `x` | `NULL` |

### 2.2.3 `paste(...)` and `paste0(...)`

- **Purpose:** String construction; returns a character vector. `paste0()` is the no-separator version (default `sep = ""`).

$$y_i = \text{paste}(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n, \text{sep} = s)$$

where each $x_i$ is recycled to the longest length, then joined by separator $s$.

### 2.2.4 `sprintf(fmt, ...)`

- **Purpose:** C-style formatted output, returning a character string.
- **Format Specifiers:**
  - `%s` — string
  - `%d` — integer
  - `%f` — fixed-point decimal
  - `%.<n>f` — `n` decimal places
  - `%e` / `%E` — scientific notation

```r
sprintf("PI = %.4f and e = %.3e", pi, exp(1))
# [1] "PI = 3.1416 and e = 2.718e+00"
```

## 2.3 File Input Functions

### 2.3.1 `read.table(file, header = FALSE, sep = "", dec = ".", ...)`

The **mother function** for rectangular data. All other readers (`read.csv`, `read.delim`, `read.delim2`) are specialized wrappers around `read.table()`.

### 2.3.2 `read.csv()` and `read.csv2()`

| Function | Separator | Decimal | Locale |
|---|---|---|---|
| `read.csv()` | `,` | `.` | US/English |
| `read.csv2()` | `;` | `,` | European |
| `read.delim()` | `\t` | `.` | Tab-delimited |
| `read.delim2()` | `\t` | `,` | European tab |

### 2.3.3 `readLines(con, n = -1, ...)`

Reads raw text lines (unparsed) — ideal for log files and unstructured text.

## 2.4 File Output Functions

### 2.4.1 `writeLines(text, con, sep = "\n", useBytes = FALSE)`

Writes a character vector one element per line.

### 2.4.2 `write.csv()` / `write.table()`

Persists data frames. Key arguments:

- `row.names = TRUE` (default!) — adds an extra leading column; usually set to `FALSE`.
- `quote = TRUE` — wraps character columns in `"`.
- `na = "NA"` — string used for missing values.

### 2.4.3 `sink(file = NULL, append = FALSE, split = FALSE, type = c("output", "message"))`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The `sink()` mechanism is heavily tested in KTU exams.** Calling `sink("log.txt")` redirects all subsequent console output (from `print`/`cat`) to the file until `sink()` is called again to restore stdout.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Function | Direction | Target | Return Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| `readline()` | In | Keyboard | Character scalar |
| `scan()` | In | Keyboard / File | Atomic vector |
| `read.table()` | In | Rectangular file | `data.frame` |
| `read.csv()` | In | CSV file | `data.frame` |
| `readLines()` | In | Text file | Character vector |
| `print()` | Out | Console | Invisible `x` |
| `cat()` | Out | Console / File | `NULL` |
| `paste()` | Out | Memory | Character vector |
| `sprintf()` | Out | Memory | Character scalar |
| `writeLines()` | Out | Connection | Invisible `NULL` |
| `write.csv()` | Out | CSV file | Invisible `NULL` |
| `sink()` | Redirect | Stdout | Invisible `NULL` |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a typical **data analytics pipeline** (e.g., IoT sensor analytics, manufacturing quality control):

- **Input phase:** `read.csv()` pulls CSV exports from SCADA systems; `scan()` ingests binary stream data; `readLines()` parses log streams.
- **Output phase:** `sprintf()` generates formatted compliance reports; `write.csv()` archives processed datasets; `sink()` captures full session transcripts for audit trails.

This I/O layer forms the **ETL (Extract–Transform–Load)** backbone of any R-driven analytics solution deployed in industrial engineering contexts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step: Working with `scan()` — Full Numerical Walkthrough

**Problem:** Read 6 floating-point numbers from a file `data.txt` and compute their arithmetic mean.

**Contents of `data.txt`:**
```
12.5
14.0
9.8
15.2
11.3
13.7
```

**Step 1 — Invoke `scan()` with explicit numeric placeholder:**

```r
values <- scan(file = "data.txt", what = double())
```

**Step 2 — Internal parsing by R:**

$$
\text{For each line } i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}: \quad v_i = \text{parse}(L_i)
$$

where $L_i$ is the i-th line token and `parse()` is R's numeric parser. If parsing fails, the line is treated as missing (assigned `NA`) and a warning is issued.

**Step 3 — Compute the mean using the canonical formula:**

$$
\bar{v} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical substitution with n = 6:**

$$
\bar{v} = \frac{12.5 + 14.0 + 9.8 + 15.2 + 11.3 + 13.7}{6}
$$

$$
\bar{v} = \frac{76.5}{6}
$$

$$
\bar{v} = 12.75
$$

**Step 5 — R implementation:**

```r
values <- scan(file = "data.txt", what = double())
mean_val <- mean(values)
sprintf("Mean = %.2f", mean_val)
# [1] "Mean = 12.75"
```

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: `sprintf()` Format Derivation

**Problem:** Print the value of $\pi$ to exactly 6 decimal places, right-aligned in a field of width 10.

**Step 1 — Identify format specifiers:**

$$
\text{FORMAT} = \% + \text{flag} + \text{width} + \text{precision} + \text{type}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the components:**

- Flag: `-` for left-align (omit for right-align default)
- Width: `10`
- Precision: `6`
- Type: `f` for fixed-point decimal

Resulting specifier: `%10.6f`

**Step 3 — R call:**

```r
sprintf("%10.6f", pi)
# [1] "  3.141593"
```

**Step 4 — Verify by counting characters:**

```
"  3.141593"
 ^^---------- two leading spaces for right-alignment
        ^--- six digits after decimal
```

The output length is exactly 10 characters: $2_{\text{spaces}} + 1_{\text{digit}} + 1_{\text{dot}} + 6_{\text{decimals}} = 10 \checkmark$

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Building a CSV File Programmatically with `sink()`

**Problem:** Generate a CSV file `report.csv` containing a header and 3 data rows without using `write.csv()`.

**Step 1 — Open the sink:**

```r
sink("report.csv")
```

After this call, **all subsequent output goes to the file** instead of the console.

**Step 2 — Write the header using `cat()`:**

```r
cat("Name,Score,Grade\n")
```

**Step 3 — Write each row:**

```r
cat("Alice,92,A\n")
cat("Bob,85,B\n")
cat("Clara,78,C\n")
```

**Step 4 — Close the sink (mandatory!):**

```r
sink()
```

**Step 5 — Verify file contents:**

```r
report <- readLines("report.csv")
# [1] "Name,Score,Grade" "Alice,92,A"       "Bob,85,B"
# [4] "Clara,78,C"
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Failure Mode:** Forgetting the final `sink()` call leaves the file *open indefinitely*. The next interactive `print()` statement in the session would still be silently redirected to the file — a notoriously common KTU lab evaluation pitfall.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right I/O Function

| Scenario | Recommended Function | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Read a single user-entered name | `readline()` | Simplest, returns 1 string |
| Read 100 numbers from a file | `scan()` | Vectorized, fast, no parsing overhead |
| Load a CSV into a data frame | `read.csv()` | Auto header, `stringsAsFactors=FALSE` default in R ≥ 4.0 |
| Print a data frame for inspection | `print()` | Respects column classes, dims |
| Concatenate heterogeneous strings | `cat()` | Variadic, no quotes |
| Build a sentence from many strings | `paste()` | Returns a string for further manipulation |
| Build a formatted report string | `sprintf()` | C-style precision control |
| Persist a data frame to disk | `write.csv()` | Industry standard, UTF-8 default |
| Capture full console session | `sink()` | Bulk redirection mechanism |
| Read raw log lines | `readLines()` | Unstructured access |

## 3.5 Worked Example: Full Pipeline

```r
# 1. INPUT from user
name <- readline("Enter student name: ")
m1   <- as.numeric(readline("Enter mark 1: "))
m2   <- as.numeric(readline("Enter mark 2: "))
m3   <- as.numeric(readline("Enter mark 3: "))

# 2. PROCESS
total <- m1 + m2 + m3
avg   <- total / 3
grade <- ifelse(avg >= 90, "A",
            ifelse(avg >= 75, "B",
              ifelse(avg >= 50, "C", "F")))

# 3. OUTPUT to console
cat("--- Report Card ---\n")
cat(sprintf("Name  : %s\n",  name))
cat(sprintf("Total : %.2f\n", total))
cat(sprintf("Average: %.2f\n", avg))
cat(sprintf("Grade : %s\n",   grade))
```

**Sample Run:**

```
Enter student name: Rahul
Enter mark 1: 85
Enter mark 2: 90
Enter mark 3: 78
--- Report Card ---
Name  : Rahul
Total : 253.00
Average: 84.33
Grade : B
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 R I/O Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[User Keyboard stdin] --> nodeB[readline single line]
    nodeA --> nodeC[scan vectorized input]
    nodeD[Flat Files csv txt dat] --> nodeE[read.table general rectangular]
    nodeD --> nodeF[read.csv csv wrapper]
    nodeD --> nodeG[readLines raw text]
    nodeH[URLs Pipes Connections] --> nodeI[Connection object via file gzurl]
    nodeB --> nodeJ[R Workspace Environment]
    nodeC --> nodeJ
    nodeE --> nodeJ
    nodeF --> nodeJ
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK[print object aware dispatcher]
    nodeJ --> nodeL[cat raw concatenation]
    nodeJ --> nodeM[paste paste0 string builder]
    nodeJ --> nodeN[sprintf C style formatting]
    nodeK --> nodeO[Console stdout]
    nodeL --> nodeO
    nodeM --> nodeP[Character Vector Returned]
    nodeN --> nodeP
    nodeO --> nodeQ[sink redirection optional]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[External File log txt]
    nodeJ --> nodeS[write.csv write.table persistence]
    nodeS --> nodeR
```

## 4.2 REPL Execution Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Prompt greater than sign] --> nodeB[Read user expression]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Parse valid syntax}
    nodeC -- No --> nodeD[Error message]
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeE[Evaluate in environment]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Implicit print call]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Display result on console]
    nodeG --> nodeA
```

## 4.3 `scan()` Internal State Machine

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[scan invoked] --> nodeB{file specified}
    nodeB -- No --> nodeC[Read from stdin keyboard]
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeD[Open file connection]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Tokenize by separator]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF{what type matches}
    nodeF -- Yes --> nodeG[Append to output vector]
    nodeF -- No --> nodeH[Assign NA warn user]
    nodeG --> nodeI{nmax reached or EOF}
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI -- No --> nodeE
    nodeI -- Yes --> nodeJ[Return typed vector]
```

## 4.4 `sink()` Redirection Lifecycle

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Normal stdout] --> nodeB[sink file path called]
    nodeB --> nodeC[All print cat redirected]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Work complete}
    nodeD -- No --> nodeC
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeE[sink called no args]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Restore stdout]
    nodeF --> nodeA
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between `print()` and `cat()` functions in R with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `print()` | `cat()` |
|---|---|---|
| Argument count | Single object | Variadic `...` |
| Quotes on strings | Yes (default `quote=TRUE`) | No |
| Vector indices `[1]` | Shown | Hidden |
| Newline | None auto-added | None auto-added |
| Return value | Invisibly returns `x` | Returns `NULL` |

```r
x <- c("apple", "banana")
print(x)    # [1] "apple"  "banana"
cat(x)      # apple banana
```

`print()` is an **S3 generic dispatching function** that adapts its output based on the class of `x`, whereas `cat()` is a low-level textual writer that simply concatenates raw characters.

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
**Explain the use of `readline()` and `scan()` functions. When would you prefer one over the other?**

**Model Answer:**

`readline()` reads **exactly one line** as a character string; ideal for interactive prompts requiring type conversion.

`scan()` reads **multiple tokens** into a typed vector; ideal for bulk numeric data.

Preference rule:
- **Single textual entry** → `readline()`
- **Multiple numeric entries / file-based bulk data** → `scan()`

```r
# Interactive single value
name <- readline("Name: ")

# Bulk numeric input
scores <- scan(what = double())
```

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Write an R program that reads a student's name and three subject marks from the console, computes the total, average, and grade (A/B/C/F as per standard cutoffs), and prints the formatted result using `cat()` and `sprintf()`.

**Model Solution:**

```r
name <- readline("Enter student name: ")
m1   <- as.numeric(readline("Enter mark 1: "))
m2   <- as.numeric(readline("Enter mark 2: "))
m3   <- as.numeric(readline("Enter mark 3: "))

total <- m1 + m2 + m3
avg   <- total / 3

grade <- if (avg >= 90) "A"
        else if (avg >= 75) "B"
        else if (avg >= 50) "C"
        else "F"

cat("\n----- Report Card -----\n")
cat(sprintf("Name    : %s\n",  name))
cat(sprintf("Mark 1  : %.2f\n", m1))
cat(sprintf("Mark 2  : %.2f\n", m2))
cat(sprintf("Mark 3  : %.2f\n", m3))
cat(sprintf("Total   : %.2f\n", total))
cat(sprintf("Average : %.2f\n", avg))
cat(sprintf("Grade   : %s\n",   grade))
```

**Explanation of Key Constructs:**

- `as.numeric()` converts the character output of `readline()` into a numeric vector; without it, addition would yield a character concatenation error.
- `sprintf("%s", name)` safely embeds the variable without quotes.
- `%.2f` enforces 2 decimal places — addresses the precision requirement commonly asked in KTU valuation.
- `cat("\n...\n")` provides newline control since `cat()` does not auto-terminate lines.

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Demonstrate the use of the `sink()` function to capture the output of an R session into a file named `session_log.txt`. Provide the complete code.

**Model Solution:**

```r
# Redirect output
sink("session_log.txt")

# All subsequent cat/print goes to file
cat("=== Session Started ===\n")
x <- c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50)
print(mean(x))
print(summary(x))
cat("Mean =", mean(x), "\n")

# Restore console output
sink()

# Verification
cat("--- File contents ---\n")
cat(readLines("session_log.txt"), sep = "\n")
```

**Expected `session_log.txt` contents:**

```
=== Session Started ===
[1] 30
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
     10      20      30      30      40      50 
Mean = 30 
```

**Critical Points:**

- The file persists until `sink()` is called again with no argument.
- Nested `sink()` calls are supported and stack-based.
- Forgetting the closing `sink()` causes silent session corruption — a top KTU viva trap.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the differences between `read.table()`, `read.csv()`, and `readLines()` with examples.

**Model Solution:**

| Function | Best For | Separator | Returns |
|---|---|---|---|
| `read.table()` | Generic rectangular data | Configurable | `data.frame` |
| `read.csv()` | Comma-separated files | `,` (fixed) | `data.frame` |
| `readLines()` | Raw line-by-line access | None (line boundary) | Character vector |

**Example 1 — `read.csv()`:**

```r
df <- read.csv("students.csv", header = TRUE, stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
head(df, 3)
#   Name  Age  Score
# 1 Anil   21     85
# 2 Beena  22     90
# 3 Cyril  20     78
```

**Example 2 — `read.table()` with custom separator:**

```r
df2 <- read.table("data_pipe.txt", sep = "|", header = TRUE)
```

**Example 3 — `readLines()`:**

```r
raw <- readLines("server.log", n = 5)
# Returns first 5 lines as character vector, no parsing.
```

**Key Differentiator:** `read.table()` and `read.csv()` perform *type inference* (numeric, character, factor) per column, while `readLines()` returns *unparsed character strings* preserving original formatting.

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Write an R script to read a numeric vector from a file using `scan()`, filter values greater than the mean, and write the filtered output to a new CSV file using `write.csv()`.

**Model Solution:**

```r
# Step 1: Read numeric data
data <- scan(file = "input_numbers.txt", what = double())

# Step 2: Compute mean
mu <- mean(data)

# Step 3: Filter values above mean
above_avg <- data[data > mu]

# Step 4: Convert to data frame for CSV writing
result_df <- data.frame(Value = above_avg)

# Step 5: Write to CSV
write.csv(result_df, file = "above_average.csv", row.names = FALSE)

# Step 6: Confirmation message to console
cat(sprintf("Mean: %.3f | Values above mean: %d\n", mu, length(above_avg)))
```

**Explanation:**

- `scan()` with `what = double()` forces numeric parsing; non-numeric tokens become `NA`.
- Boolean indexing `data[data > mu]` filters efficiently in one line.
- `data.frame(Value = above_avg)` wraps the vector into a columnar structure required by `write.csv()`.
- `row.names = FALSE` prevents R from adding a redundant index column to the output — a frequent cause of mark deduction.

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Triggers (KTU Examiner's Eye):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `as.numeric()` after `readline()`** — leads to character concatenation (`"85" + "90"` → `Error`). *Penalty: 2 marks.*
> 2. **Not closing `sink()`** — file remains open, next `print()` writes to file silently. *Penalty: 1 mark for incomplete output.*
> 3. **Using `row.names = TRUE` (default) in `write.csv()`** — pollutes data with an unnamed index column. *Penalty: 1 mark for unclean output.*
> 4. **Confusing `cat()` with `print()` semantics** — expecting `cat(x)` to show quotes or `[1]`. *Penalty: conceptual marks.*
> 5. **Mixing `sep` and `dec` in European locales** — using `read.csv()` on `;`-separated files yields single-column data. *Penalty: 2 marks.*
> 6. **Not using `sprintf()` for precision control** — printing `pi` without format specifier loses marks on "formatted output" questions.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **`readline()`** returns a **character** string — wrap with `as.numeric()` / `as.integer()` for math.
- **`scan()`** returns a **typed vector** based on the `what` argument; supports files and stdin.
- **`print()`** is class-aware (S3 dispatch); `cat()` is raw and quote-free.
- **`paste()`** joins with separator; `paste0()` is the no-separator variant.
- **`sprintf()`** uses C-style format specifiers: `%s`, `%d`, `%f`, `%.<n>f`, `%e`.
- **`read.table()`** is the universal reader; `read.csv()` / `read.delim()` are thin wrappers.
- **`readLines()`** gives raw, unparsed character lines.
- **`write.csv()`** always sets `row.names = FALSE` in exam answers to avoid the default index column.
- **`sink()`** redirects stdout; **must be closed** by a second `sink()` call.
- The **REPL** auto-prints at the prompt, but **function bodies** require explicit `print()` / `cat()` for output.
- **Encoding caveat**: Always check `fileEncoding = "UTF-8"` for cross-platform CSV files.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reading and Writing Files
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET862 - PROGRAMMING AND DATA ANALYTICS WITH R
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reading and Writing Files

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**File I/O (Input/Output)** in R refers to the systematic process of establishing a communication channel between the in-memory R workspace (RAM) and persistent secondary storage devices (HDD/SSD), enabling data to be **imported (read)** for analysis or **exported (written)** for archival, sharing, and reporting. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme, this concept falls under **Data Acquisition and Wrangling**, a foundational competency for any data analytics pipeline.

R treats files as **connections** — abstract streams that can be opened, queried, read from, written to, and closed. A connection in R is a flexible object that supports multiple file encodings, compression formats, and remote protocols (HTTP/FTP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 2):** Students must master the base R functions (`read.csv`, `write.table`, `readLines`, `writeLines`) and the **tidyverse equivalents** (`readr::read_csv`, `readr::write_csv`, `readxl::read_excel`) — the latter being the **industry-standard** approach for modern analytics.

### 1.2 Core Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Connection** | An R object representing an open link to a file/URL/socket |
| **Working Directory** | Default folder where R looks for files and saves outputs |
| **Delimiter** | Character separating columns (e.g., comma `,`, tab `\t`, semicolon `;`) |
| **Header** | First row of a tabular file containing column names |
| **Encoding** | Character encoding standard (UTF-8, Latin-1, ASCII) |
| **Path** | File location: **absolute** (`C:/data/file.csv`) or **relative** (`data/file.csv`) |
| **Tibble** | A modern data frame returned by `readr` — friendlier than base R data frames |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Post Office" Model

Think of file I/O in R like a **post office**:

- **Opening a file** is like opening a **post office box**: you reserve a dedicated channel.
- **Reading** is like **receiving mail** from the box into your hands (RAM).
- **Writing** is like **depositing a letter** into the box for someone to collect later.
- **Closing the file** is like **returning the key** — forgetting this is like leaving the box open, which can corrupt data or lock others out.

In a real data analytics project, raw data is like a **shipping container arriving at a port** — too big to view all at once. R opens a connection, unloads the relevant pallets (rows), and stores them in warehouses (data frames/tibbles) for inspection.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Conceptual Hook:** Examiners love asking: *"Why do we need to explicitly close file connections in R?"* — Answer: To release system resources, flush buffered writes, and prevent file corruption or memory leaks.

### 1.4 Types of Data Sources R Can Read

1. **Flat files** — `.csv`, `.tsv`, `.txt` (delimited)
2. **Excel workbooks** — `.xls`, `.xlsx` (requires `readxl` or `openxlsx`)
3. **Statistical software files** — `.sas7bdat`, `.sav` (SPSS), `.dta` (Stata) via `haven`
4. **Databases** — SQL via `DBI` + `RSQLite`/`RMySQL`/`RPostgres`
5. **Web/JSON/XML** — via `jsonlite`, `xml2`
6. **Compressed files** — `.gz`, `.bz2`, `.zip`, `.tar` (transparent handling)
7. **Remote URLs** — pass a URL string directly to most reader functions

### 1.5 The Three Pillars of File I/O Workflow

$$
\text{R File I/O} = \underbrace{\text{Locate}}_{\text{Path/Directory}} + \underbrace{\text{Open}}_{\text{Connection}} + \underbrace{\text{Operate}}_{\text{Read/Write}}
$$

- **Locate** → Tell R *where* the file is using a path.
- **Open** → R establishes a connection (often automatic in high-level functions).
- **Operate** → Perform `read.*` or `write.*` operations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The File I/O Conceptual Stack

File reading in R is layered as follows (from low-level to high-level):

1. **Hardware Layer** — Physical disk sectors
2. **OS Layer** — File descriptors, permissions
3. **R Connection Layer** — R's `connection` object (`file()`, `gzfile()`, `url()`)
4. **I/O Functions** — `readLines()`, `writeLines()`, `readBin()`, `writeBin()`
5. **Parser Layer** — `read.table()`, `read.csv()` (base R)
6. **Tidy Layer** — `readr::read_csv()`, `readxl::read_excel()` (modern)

> [!TIP]
> KTU questions typically test Layers 5 and 6. The tidyverse functions are **10x–100x faster** than base R equivalents and produce tibbles with better defaults.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet: Function Reference Table

| Function | Package | Purpose | Key Arguments |
|---|---|---|---|
| `read.csv(file, header = TRUE, sep = ",")` | base | Read CSV → data frame | `file`, `header`, `sep`, `dec`, `na.strings` |
| `read.table(file, header, sep)` | base | Generic delimited reader | `file`, `header`, `sep`, `quote` |
| `read_delim(file, delim, col_names)` | readr | Tidyverse delimited reader | `file`, `delim`, `col_names`, `na`, `skip` |
| `read_csv(file)` | readr | Tidyverse CSV reader (fast) | `file`, `col_types`, `locale` |
| `read_excel(path, sheet)` | readxl | Read `.xlsx`/`.xls` | `path`, `sheet`, `range`, `col_names` |
| `write.csv(x, file, row.names)` | base | Write CSV from data frame | `x`, `file`, `row.names` |
| `write_csv(x, file)` | readr | Tidyverse CSV writer | `x`, `file`, `na`, `append` |
| `writeLines(text, con)` | base | Write character vector line-by-line | `text`, `con` |
| `readLines(con, n)` | base | Read file as character vector | `con`, `n` (max lines) |
| `file(description, open)` | base | Create a file connection | `description`, `open` mode |
| `getwd()` / `setwd(dir)` | base | Get/set working directory | `dir` |
| `list.files(path, pattern)` | base | List files matching pattern | `path`, `pattern`, `full.names` |
| `file.path(..., fsep)` | base | Construct OS-safe path | component strings |
| `fread(input, sep)` | data.table | Ultra-fast CSV reader | `input`, `sep`, `header` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **File Open Modes (`open` argument):** `"r"` = read, `"w"` = write (truncates), `"a"` = append, `"rb"`/`"wb"` = binary read/write. Use `"r"` for input, `"w"` or `"a"` for output.

### 2.3 The Connection Model — In-Depth

R uses a unified connection abstraction. A connection is essentially a wrapper around a file descriptor.

**General Syntax:**

```r
con <- file(description = "path/to/file", open = "r")
data <- readLines(con, n = 10)   # read first 10 lines
close(con)
```

**Alternate Connection Types:**

```r
con <- gzfile("data.csv.gz", "rt")     # gzip-compressed text
con <- bzfile("data.csv.bz2", "rt")    # bzip2-compressed text
con <- url("https://example.com/data.csv", "r")  # HTTP URL
con <- pipe("awk '{print $1}' file.txt", "r")   # shell pipe
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why connections matter for KTU:** They allow streaming (reading huge files line-by-line without loading everything into memory) and remote access without intermediate downloads.

### 2.4 The `readr` Package — Modern Best Practice

The `readr` package is part of the **tidyverse** and provides:

- **Faster parsing** (C++ backend via `Rcpp`)
- **Smarter type guessing** with progress bars
- **Tibble output** (prints cleanly, no row-name surprises)
- **Reproducible type specifications** via `col_types`

**Function Family:**

$$
\text{readr} = \left\{ \text{read\_csv}, \text{read\_tsv}, \text{read\_delim}, \text{read\_fwf}, \text{read\_log}, \text{read\_csv2} \right\}
$$

**`read_csv` argument structure:**

```r
read_csv(
  file,         # path or URL
  col_names = TRUE,    # TRUE / FALSE / character vector
  col_types = NULL,    # list of column types (cols())
  na = c("", "NA"),    # strings to interpret as NA
  skip = 0,            # rows to skip at start
  n_max = Inf,         # max rows to read
  progress = TRUE      # show progress bar
)
```

### 2.5 The `readxl` Package — Excel Files

R's base cannot read Excel natively. You need either `readxl` (tidyverse, read-only) or `openxlsx` (read + write).

```r
library(readxl)
df <- read_excel(
  path = "sales.xlsx",
  sheet = "Q1_Data",   # sheet name or number
  range = "A1:D100",   # optional cell range
  col_names = TRUE
)
```

### 2.6 Reading Data from URLs and Compressed Files

Most reader functions accept **URLs** directly:

```r
df <- read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dataset/iris.csv")
```

Compressed files are handled **transparently**:

```r
df <- read_csv("data.csv.gz")    # works without manual decompression
```

### 2.7 Writing to Files

**Base R approach:**

```r
write.csv(mtcars, file = "output.csv", row.names = FALSE)
write.table(df, file = "output.txt", sep = "\t", quote = FALSE)
```

**Tidyverse approach (faster, no row names by default):**

```r
library(readr)
write_csv(mtcars, "output.csv", na = "NA", append = FALSE, col_names = TRUE)
```

**Writing character vectors line-by-line:**

```r
con <- file("log.txt", "w")
writeLines(c("Line 1", "Line 2", "Line 3"), con)
close(con)
```

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | File I/O Use Case |
|---|---|
| **IoT / Embedded Systems** | Reading sensor logs from `.csv` for time-series analysis |
| **Biostatistics** | Importing patient records from Excel/CSV |
| **Civil Engineering** | Reading survey data, material test results |
| **Machine Learning** | Loading training datasets, exporting predictions |
| **Web Scraping** | Saving scraped HTML/JSON to local files for offline parsing |
| **Reproducible Research** | Saving cleaned data + RMarkdown reports |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **complete, executable R code walkthroughs** for the most examination-relevant file I/O operations. Every step is annotated.

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Reading a CSV File (Base R)

**Problem:** Read the file `students.csv` located in the current working directory and display its structure.

**Step 1 — Verify the working directory.**

```r
getwd()
# [1] "C:/Users/Student/Documents"
```

**Step 2 — List files to confirm the file exists.**

```r
list.files(pattern = "\\.csv$")
# [1] "students.csv"  "grades.csv"
```

**Step 3 — Read the CSV file using `read.csv()`.**

```r
students <- read.csv(
  file = "students.csv",
  header = TRUE,        # first row contains column names
  sep = ",",            # comma-separated
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE  # don't auto-convert strings to factors
)
```

**Step 4 — Inspect the result.**

```r
str(students)
# 'data.frame':  100 obs. of  5 variables:
#  $ id       : int  1 2 3 4 5 ...
#  $ name     : chr  "Alice" "Bob" "Charlie" ...
#  $ age      : int  20 21 22 19 20 ...
#  $ grade    : chr  "A" "B+" "A-" "B" ...
#  $ gpa      : num  3.8 3.2 3.9 3.0 3.5 ...
```

**Step 5 — Confirm dimensions and preview.**

```r
dim(students)        # [1] 100   5
head(students, 3)
#   id    name age grade gpa
# 1  1   Alice  20     A 3.8
# 2  2     Bob  21    B+ 3.2
# 3  3 Charlie  22    A- 3.9
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Note:** The default behavior of `read.csv` includes `row.names = NULL`. The base function always adds an unwanted row index column when written with `write.csv(df, file)` unless you set `row.names = FALSE`.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Reading CSV Using `readr` (Tidyverse)

**Step 1 — Install and load the package.**

```r
install.packages("readr")   # do this once
library(readr)
```

**Step 2 — Read the file with explicit type specification.**

```r
students <- read_csv(
  file = "students.csv",
  col_types = cols(
    id    = col_integer(),
    name  = col_character(),
    age   = col_integer(),
    grade = col_factor(levels = c("A+", "A", "A-", "B+", "B", "B-", "C", "D", "F")),
    gpa   = col_double()
  ),
  na = c("", "NA", "NULL")    # treat these strings as missing
)
```

**Step 3 — Verify it returns a tibble.**

```r
class(students)
# [1] "spec_tbl_df" "tbl_df"      "tbl"         "data.frame"
```

**Step 4 — Print the parsing specification (a tidyverse diagnostic).**

```r
spec(students)
# cols(
#   id    = col_integer(),
#   name  = col_character(),
#   age   = col_integer(),
#   grade = col_factor(levels = c(...), ordered = FALSE),
#   gpa   = col_double()
# )
```

> [!TIP]
> The `spec()` output is a **reproducible parsing recipe** — re-running with the same `col_types` guarantees identical data types across systems. KTU examiners appreciate this concept of *reproducibility*.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Reading an Excel File

**Step 1 — Install and load `readxl`.**

```r
install.packages("readxl")
library(readxl)
```

**Step 2 — Inspect available sheets before reading.**

```r
excel_sheets("sales_data.xlsx")
# [1] "Q1" "Q2" "Q3" "Q4"
```

**Step 3 — Read a specific sheet.**

```r
q1_sales <- read_excel(
  path  = "sales_data.xlsx",
  sheet = "Q1",
  range = "A1:F200",          # limit cell range
  col_names = TRUE,
  na = ""
)
```

**Step 4 — Optional: read all sheets in a loop.**

```r
sheets <- excel_sheets("sales_data.xlsx")
all_data <- lapply(sheets, function(s) {
  read_excel("sales_data.xlsx", sheet = s)
})
names(all_data) <- sheets
q1 <- all_data$Q1
```

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4: Writing Data to Files

**Step 1 — Write a data frame to CSV (base R).**

```r
write.csv(
  x = mtcars,
  file = "mtcars_output.csv",
  row.names = FALSE     # CRITICAL: prevents unwanted row numbers
)
```

**Step 2 — Write using `readr` (recommended).**

```r
library(readr)
write_csv(
  x = mtcars,
  file = "mtcars_output.csv",
  na = "NA",
  append = FALSE,      # FALSE = overwrite, TRUE = append
  col_names = TRUE
)
```

**Step 3 — Write a delimited text file with custom separator.**

```r
write.table(
  x = mtcars,
  file = "mtcars_output.tsv",
  sep = "\t",
  quote = FALSE,
  row.names = FALSE,
  na = ""
)
```

**Step 4 — Write a log file line-by-line.**

```r
log_lines <- c(
  paste("Analysis started at:", Sys.time()),
  paste("Total records processed:", nrow(mtcars)),
  "Mean mpg: 20.09",
  "Analysis complete."
)
con <- file("analysis_log.txt", open = "w")
writeLines(log_lines, con)
close(con)
```

**Step 5 — Verify by re-reading.**

```r
readLines("analysis_log.txt")
```

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 5: Streaming a Large File with Connections

For files too large to fit in memory, use **connections** to read in chunks.

```r
con <- file("huge_dataset.csv", open = "r")
chunk_size <- 1000
repeat {
  lines <- readLines(con, n = chunk_size)
  if (length(lines) == 0) break   # EOF reached
  # process chunk here
  cat("Processed", length(lines), "lines\n")
}
close(con)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This pattern is **critical in production data engineering** — KTU may ask conceptual questions on why `read.csv()` is unsuitable for very large files (it loads everything into RAM).

---

### 3.6 Worked Example 6: Building Robust File Paths

**Use `file.path()` for cross-platform safety (handles `/` vs `\`):**

```r
data_dir <- "C:/Users/Student/Documents"
sub_dir  <- "Projects"
filename <- "results.csv"

full_path <- file.path(data_dir, sub_dir, filename, fsep = .Platform$file.sep)
# On Windows: "C:/Users/Student/Documents/Projects/results.csv"
# On Linux:   "C:/Users/Student/Documents/Projects/results.csv"
```

**Use `here::here()` for project-relative paths (best practice):**

```r
install.packages("here")
library(here)
here("data", "raw", "input.csv")
# Always resolves to <project_root>/data/raw/input.csv
```

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Function

| Scenario | Recommended Function | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Small CSV, base R only | `read.csv()` | No extra packages needed |
| Large CSV, performance critical | `data.table::fread()` | Fastest in R |
| Standard tidyverse workflow | `readr::read_csv()` | Tibble, fast, reproducible |
| Excel file (read only) | `readxl::read_excel()` | No Java dependency, simple |
| Excel file (read + write) | `openxlsx::read.xlsx` / `write.xlsx` | Full Excel control |
| Tab-separated, base R | `read.delim()` | `sep = "\t"` by default |
| Fixed-width files | `readr::read_fwf()` or `read.fwf()` | No delimiter |
| Huge file, low memory | `file()` + `readLines(n=)` loop | Stream processing |
| Remote URL | `read_csv(url)` | Transparent HTTP support |
| Compressed file | `read_csv("file.csv.gz")` | Auto-decompression |
| JSON data | `jsonlite::fromJSON()` | Nested structure support |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: R File I/O Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: User wants data] --> B{Data source type?}
    B -->|CSV/TSV/TXT| C[Use read.csv or read_csv]
    B -->|Excel| D[Use readxl read_excel]
    B -->|Database| E[Use DBI + RSQLite]
    B -->|Web API| F[Use jsonlite or httr]
    B -->|URL/HTTP| G[Pass URL string directly]
    C --> H{File size check}
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H -->|Small less than 100 MB| I[Load fully into data frame]
    H -->|Large greater than 1 GB| J[Stream via file connection]
    I --> K[Clean and transform data]
    J --> K
    K --> L{Output required?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Use write.csv or write_csv]
    L -->|No| N[In-memory analysis only]
    M --> O[End: Data persisted to disk]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Close all open connections]
    style A fill:E3F2FD,stroke:1976D2,color:000
    style P fill:C8E6C9,stroke:388E3C,color:000
    style H fill:FFF9C4,stroke:F57C00,color:000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Function Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Need to read a file] --> Q1{Is it Excel .xlsx or .xls?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| R1[readxl package]
    R1 --> R2[read_excel function]
    R1 --> R3[excel_sheets to list sheets]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is it delimited CSV TSV TXT?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Using tidyverse?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| R4[readr read_csv read_tsv read_delim]
    Q3 -->|No| R5[Base R read.csv read.table read.delim]
    Q2 -->|No| Q4{Is it compressed .gz .bz2 .zip?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| R6[read_csv handles automatically]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{Is it a remote URL?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| R7[read_csv with URL string]
    Q5 -->|No| Q6{Is it JSON XML or HTML?}
    Q6 -->|Yes| R8[jsonlite or xml2 package]
    Q6 -->|No| R9[Use file connection + readLines]
    style S fill:BBDEFB,stroke:1565C0,color:000
    style R2 fill:C8E6C9,stroke:2E7D32,color:000
    style R4 fill:C8E6C9,stroke:2E7D32,color:000
    style R5 fill:FFECB3,stroke:F57C00,color:000
    style R8 fill:C8E6C9,stroke:2E7D32,color:000
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Connection Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Closed
    Closed --> Opening: file path, open mode
    Opening --> Open: connection established
    Open --> Reading: readLines or read.csv
    Open --> Writing: writeLines or write.csv
    Open --> Appending: append mode
    Reading --> Open
    Writing --> Open
    Appending --> Open
    Open --> Closing: close function
    Reading --> Closing: EOF reached
    Writing --> Closing: flush complete
    Closing --> Closed: resource released
    Closed --> [*]
    note right of Open
        Connection holds:
        - file descriptor
        - buffer state
        - encoding
    end note
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: readr Parsing Pipeline (Internal)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw file bytes] --> B[Lazy file reader]
    B --> C[Type guessing engine]
    C --> D{Column type}
    D -->|Integer| E[parse_integer]
    D -->|Double| F[parse_double]
    D -->|Character| G[parse_character]
    D -->|Date| H[parse_date]
    D -->|Logical| I[parse_logical]
    E --> J[Tibble output]
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Ready for analysis]
    style A fill:E1F5FE,stroke:0277BD,color:000
    style J fill:C8E6C9,stroke:2E7D32,color:000
    style K fill:FFF59D,stroke:F9A825,color:000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Q: Differentiate between `read.csv()` and `read_csv()` in R. Mention the package each belongs to.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `read.csv()` (Base R) | `read_csv()` (readr/tidyverse) |
|---|---|---|
| **Package** | `utils` (base R, pre-installed) | `readr` (requires installation) |
| **Output** | Base `data.frame` | `tibble` (`tbl_df`) |
| **Speed** | Slower (R-level parsing) | Faster (C++ backend via Rcpp) |
| **Row names** | Often gets unwanted row index | No row names by default |
| **Progress bar** | None | Shows progress for large files |
| **Type control** | `colClasses` | `col_types` with `cols()` helper |
| **String conversion** | Auto-converts to factor (older R) | Keeps as character |

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`

**Q: Explain the purpose of `close()` function after opening a file connection in R. What happens if it is not called?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `close()` function terminates the open file connection and releases the associated system resources (file descriptor, memory buffer, OS handle). It also **flushes any buffered data** to disk, ensuring all pending writes are committed.

**Consequences of not closing:**
1. **Memory leak** — File descriptor remains held, consuming OS resources.
2. **Data loss/corruption** — Buffered writes may never reach disk if the process is terminated.
3. **File locking** — On some OSes (Windows), the file remains locked and cannot be modified by other processes.
4. **Connection limit** — R has a default limit of 128 open connections; not closing exhausts this quickly.

**Example:**

```r
con <- file("data.txt", "w")
writeLines(c("Hello", "World"), con)
close(con)   # mandatory step
```

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the different file connection types available in R. Write R code to read a compressed `.csv.gz` file using a connection and display its first 5 rows. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Discuss the `readr` package with its key functions. Compare `read_csv()` with `read_csv2()` and explain the role of `col_types` argument with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — File Connections in R (7 marks)

**Types of Connections in R:**

R supports a unified connection model that abstracts various data sources. The main connection-opening functions are:

| Function | Use Case | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `file()` | Plain text/binary files | `con <- file("data.txt", "r")` |
| `gzfile()` | Gzip-compressed files | `con <- gzfile("data.csv.gz", "rt")` |
| `bzfile()` | Bzip2-compressed files | `con <- bzfile("data.csv.bz2", "rt")` |
| `xzfile()` | XZ-compressed files | `con <- xzfile("data.csv.xz", "rt")` |
| `url()` | Web URLs (HTTP/FTP) | `con <- url("https://site.com/data.csv", "r")` |
| `pipe()` | Shell command pipes | `con <- pipe("cat data.csv", "r")` |
| `textConnection()` | In-memory string as file | `con <- textConnection(string_var, "r")` |
| `socketConnection()` | TCP/IP sockets | `con <- socketConnection(host, port)` |

**R Code to Read a Compressed File via Connection:**

```r
# Step 1: Open a gzip connection in text-read mode
con <- gzfile(description = "sales_data.csv.gz", open = "rt")

# Step 2: Read the first 5 lines as a character vector
header     <- readLines(con, n = 1)   # header row
data_lines <- readLines(con, n = 5)   # next 5 data rows

# Step 3: Close the connection
close(con)

# Step 4: Display
cat("Header:", header, "\n\n")
```
