- [x] **Slope formula (SLR):** $\hat{\beta}_1 = S_{xy}/S_{xx} = \dfrac{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})}{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2}$
- [x] **Intercept formula (SLR):** $\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}_1\bar{x}$ — *regression line always passes through $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$.*
- [x] **MLR normal equation (matrix form):** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{Y}$
- [x] **Interpret MLR coefficients with the phrase:** *"holding all other variables constant."*
- [x] **Five Gauss-Markov assumptions:** Linearity, Independence, Homoscedasticity, Normality, No Multicollinearity.
- [x] **Use Adjusted R²** (not R²) when comparing MLR models with different numbers of predictors.
- [x] **F-test** checks overall model significance; **t-test** checks individual coefficient significance.
- [x] **For SLR:** $F = t^2$ (the F-statistic equals the square of the t-statistic).
- [x] **Residual diagnostics:** Residuals vs Fitted (linearity, homoscedasticity), Q-Q plot (normality), Leverage plot (influential points via Cook's distance).
- [x] **In R:** `model <- lm(Y ~ X1 + X2, data = df)`; use `summary(model)` for coefficients and p-values, `plot(model)` for diagnostics, `predict(model, newdata)` for forecasting.
- [x] **Degrees of freedom:** For SLR use $n-2$; for MLR use $n-p-1$.
- [x] **A high R² does not guarantee a good model** — always check residual plots and assumption validity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Strategy (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - Part A answers should be **3–4 lines** + 1 formula + 1 example — total ≈ 5 minutes per question.
> - Part B answers should include **derivation + numerical + interpretation** — total ≈ 18–22 minutes per question.
> - For full marks in numericals, always show **three things**: the formula used, the substitution step, and the final answer with units (if applicable).
> - For theory answers, always **draw a small diagram** where possible (e.g., scatter plot with fitted line for SLR).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generalized Linear Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET862 - PROGRAMMING AND DATA ANALYTICS WITH R
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Generalized Linear Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Generalized Linear Model (GLM)** is a flexible statistical modeling framework introduced by *Nelder and Wedderburn (1972)* that unifies various regression models (linear regression, logistic regression, Poisson regression, etc.) under a single theoretical umbrella. A GLM generalizes ordinary linear regression by allowing the response variable to follow any distribution from the **exponential family** and by using a **link function** to relate the linear predictor to the mean of the response.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> A GLM is fully specified by three components:
> 1. **Random Component** – The response $Y$ belongs to the exponential family of distributions.
> 2. **Systematic Component** – The linear predictor $\eta = X\beta = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \beta_p x_p$.
> 3. **Link Function** – A monotone differentiable function $g(\cdot)$ such that $g(\mathbb{E}[Y \vert X]) = \eta$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Universal Translator":**
> Think of a GLM as a **smart universal translator** standing between your predictors (input features) and your response (output). In ordinary linear regression, the translator is rigid — it can only speak one "language" (continuous Gaussian outcomes). But a GLM is multilingual: it can translate inputs into:
> - **Yes/No outcomes** (logistic regression for binary classification — e.g., will a customer buy? *Yes* or *No*)
> - **Count outcomes** (Poisson regression — e.g., how many calls will a call-center receive per hour?)
> - **Continuous outcomes** (linear regression — e.g., predicting house prices)
>
> The **link function** acts as the translation rule — it decides *how* to convert the input signal into a form the output can understand. The **exponential family** decides what *kind* of output is allowed.

## 1.3 Why GLMs Matter in Data Analytics

GLMs are the **workhorse of predictive analytics** because real-world data rarely follows the strict Gaussian assumption:

| Real-World Problem | Response Type | Why Linear Regression Fails |
|---|---|---|
| Customer churn prediction | Binary (0/1) | Predictions can fall outside [0,1] |
| Disease incidence rate | Count (0,1,2,…) | Negative predictions are impossible |
| Insurance claim severity | Continuous, positive | Heteroscedasticity (unequal variance) |
| Survey rating | Ordinal (1–5) | Bounded, non-Gaussian |

GLMs handle **all** of these within one coherent, likelihood-based framework.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember GLM components:** **R-S-L** → **R**andom component, **S**ystematic component, **L**ink function. Always think **R-S-L** when you encounter a GLM problem.

## 1.4 Mathematical Setup

Let $Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_n$ be independent response observations, and let $X$ be the $n \times (p+1)$ design matrix with rows $x_i^T$. The GLM assumes:

$$\eta_i = x_i^T \beta = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{i1} + \beta_2 x_{i2} + \cdots + \beta_p x_{ip}$$

$$\eta_i = g(\mu_i), \quad \text{where } \mu_i = \mathbb{E}[Y_i \vert x_i]$$

and the distribution of $Y_i$ belongs to the exponential family with density:

$$f(y_i; \theta_i, \phi) = \exp\left\{\frac{y_i \theta_i - b(\theta_i)}{a(\phi)} + c(y_i, \phi)\right\}$$

where $\theta_i$ is the **natural (canonical) parameter**, $\phi$ is the **dispersion parameter**, and $b(\cdot)$, $c(\cdot)$, $a(\cdot)$ are known functions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of a GLM (Structured Logic)

### Pillar 1 — The Random Component (Exponential Family)
The response $Y$ must be a member of the **exponential family of distributions**. The single-parameter exponential family is:

$$f(y; \theta, \phi) = \exp\left\{\frac{y\theta - b(\theta)}{a(\phi)} + c(y, \phi)\right\}$$

**Key identities** derived from this density:

$$\mathbb{E}[Y] = \mu = b'(\theta) \quad \text{(mean of Y)}$$

$$\text{Var}(Y) = b''(\theta) \cdot a(\phi) \quad \text{(variance of Y)}$$

The function $V(\mu) = b''(\theta)$ is called the **variance function** — it is the part of the variance that depends on the mean.

### Pillar 2 — The Systematic Component (Linear Predictor)
This is the familiar linear combination of predictors:

$$\eta = X\beta = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \cdots + \beta_p x_p$$

It is a **purely additive, unrestricted** linear quantity that can take any real value in $(-\infty, +\infty)$.

### Pillar 3 — The Link Function
The link function $g(\cdot)$ bridges $\eta$ and $\mu$:

$$\eta = g(\mu) \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \mu = g^{-1}(\eta)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical Link Function** — A link is called *canonical* if $\eta = \theta$ (i.e., $g(\mu) = (b')^{-1}(\mu)$). It yields the simplest, most symmetric Fisher information matrix and is the default in R's `glm()` function.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table is the **essential cheat sheet** for GLM exam problems. **Memorize the structure** — every question will test a variant of these formulas.

| Distribution | Random Component | Canonical Link $g(\mu)$ | Linear Predictor | Variance Function $V(\mu)$ | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Gaussian (Normal)** | $Y \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ | **Identity:** $\eta = \mu$ | $x^T \beta = \mu$ | $V(\mu) = 1$ | Continuous data, OLS regression |
| **Binomial** | $Y \sim \text{Bin}(n, \pi)$ | **Logit:** $\eta = \log\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{1-\pi}\right)$ | $x^T \beta = \log\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{1-\pi}\right)$ | $V(\mu) = \mu(1-\mu)$ | Binary / proportion data |
| **Poisson** | $Y \sim \text{Poi}(\lambda)$ | **Log:** $\eta = \log(\lambda)$ | $x^T \beta = \log(\lambda)$ | $V(\mu) = \mu$ | Count data |
| **Gamma** | $Y \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha, \beta)$ | **Inverse:** $\eta = 1/\mu$ | $x^T \beta = 1/\mu$ | $V(\mu) = \mu^2$ | Positive skewed, e.g. insurance |
| **Inverse Gaussian** | $Y \sim \text{IG}$ | **Inverse-square:** $\eta = 1/\mu^2$ | $x^T \beta = 1/\mu^2$ | $V(\mu) = \mu^3$ | Survival / lifetime data |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked *"Which link function for logistic regression?"* — the **canonical** answer is the **logit link** $\eta = \log(\pi/(1-\pi))$. If asked for an *alternative* link for binomial GLM, mention the **probit** link $\eta = \Phi^{-1}(\pi)$ where $\Phi$ is the standard normal CDF.

## 2.3 Maximum Likelihood Estimation in GLMs

Since the observations are independent, the joint log-likelihood is:

$$\ell(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log f(y_i; \theta_i, \phi) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\{\frac{y_i \theta_i - b(\theta_i)}{a(\phi)} + c(y_i, \phi)\right\}$$

The **score function** is obtained by differentiating with respect to $\beta_j$:

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \beta_j} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{(y_i - \mu_i)}{V(\mu_i) \cdot a(\phi)} \cdot x_{ij}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The famous GLM Score Equation:** Setting $\partial \ell / \partial \beta_j = 0$ gives the **normal-like equations**:
> $$X^T W X \beta = X^T W z$$
> where $W$ is a diagonal weight matrix with $W_{ii} = 1 / (V(\mu_i) \cdot (g'(\mu_i))^2 \cdot a(\phi))$ and $z$ is the **working response / adjusted dependent variable**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | GLM Application |
|---|---|
| **Reliability Engineering** | Gamma GLM for time-to-failure data |
| **Quality Control** | Binomial GLM for pass/fail proportions |
| **Telecommunications** | Poisson GLM for call-arrival counts |
| **Biomedical Engineering** | Logistic GLM for disease classification |
| **Civil/Structural** | Gamma GLM for stress-life (S-N) modeling |

## 2.5 Model Fit Diagnostics

The **deviance** $D$ measures the discrepancy between the fitted model and the **saturated model** (a model with one parameter per observation):

$$D = 2 \left[ \ell(\hat{\beta}_{\text{sat}}) - \ell(\hat{\beta}) \right] = 2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\{ y_i (\tilde{\theta}_i - \hat{\theta}_i) - b(\tilde{\theta}_i) + b(\hat{\theta}_i) \right\}$$

where $\tilde{\theta}_i$ is the MLE under the saturated model and $\hat{\theta}_i$ is the MLE under the fitted model.

- **Null Deviance** $D_0$: deviance of the model with only an intercept.
- **Residual Deviance** $D_{\text{res}}$: deviance of the fitted model.
- **AIC** (Akaike Information Criterion): $\text{AIC} = -2\ell(\hat{\beta}) + 2p$ — used for model selection.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mean and Variance of the Exponential Family

Given the density:

$$f(y; \theta, \phi) = \exp\left\{\frac{y\theta - b(\theta)}{a(\phi)} + c(y, \phi)\right\}$$

The log-density is:

$$\log f(y; \theta, \phi) = \frac{y\theta - b(\theta)}{a(\phi)} + c(y, \phi)$$

**Step 1: Compute $\partial \log f / \partial \theta$**

$$\frac{\partial \log f}{\partial \theta} = \frac{y - b'(\theta)}{a(\phi)}$$

**Step 2: Score function expectation equals zero** (a property of MLEs from regular families).

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\frac{\partial \log f}{\partial \theta}\right] = 0 \implies \frac{\mathbb{E}[Y] - b'(\theta)}{a(\phi)} = 0 \implies \mathbb{E}[Y] = b'(\theta) = \mu$$

**Step 3: Compute $\partial^2 \log f / \partial \theta^2$** for the Fisher information.

$$\frac{\partial^2 \log f}{\partial \theta^2} = \frac{-b''(\theta)}{a(\phi)}$$

**Step 4: Use the information identity** $\text{Var}(Y) = -\mathbb{E}\left[\partial^2 \log f / \partial \theta^2\right]$:

$$\text{Var}(Y) = -\mathbb{E}\left[\frac{-b''(\theta)}{a(\phi)}\right] = \frac{b''(\theta)}{a(\phi)}$$

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\mathbb{E}[Y] = b'(\theta), \qquad \text{Var}(Y) = \frac{b''(\theta)}{a(\phi)}}$$

> **Intuition:** The function $b(\theta)$ is the *moment-generating* backbone. Differentiate once → get the mean; differentiate twice → get (the mean-dependent part of) the variance. This is the **engine** of every GLM.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Logistic Regression MLE

For a binary response $Y_i \in \{0, 1\}$ with success probability $\pi_i = P(Y_i = 1 \vert x_i)$, the **Bernoulli likelihood** for observation $i$ is:

$$f(y_i; \pi_i) = \pi_i^{y_i} (1 - \pi_i)^{1 - y_i}$$

The log-likelihood is:

$$\ell(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ y_i \log \pi_i + (1 - y_i)\log(1 - \pi_i) \right]$$

**Step 1: Apply the logit link.**

$$\eta_i = x_i^T \beta = \log\!\left(\frac{\pi_i}{1 - \pi_i}\right) \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \pi_i = \frac{e^{x_i^T \beta}}{1 + e^{x_i^T \beta}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x_i^T \beta}}$$

This last expression is the **sigmoid (logistic) function**, denoted $\sigma(\eta_i)$.

**Step 2: Substitute $\pi_i = \sigma(x_i^T \beta)$ into the log-likelihood.**

$$\ell(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ y_i \log \sigma(x_i^T \beta) + (1 - y_i)\log(1 - \sigma(x_i^T \beta)) \right]$$

**Step 3: Differentiate with respect to $\beta_j$** using the identity $\sigma'(\eta) = \sigma(\eta)(1-\sigma(\eta))$:

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \beta_j} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ y_i - \sigma(x_i^T \beta) \right] x_{ij} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \pi_i) x_{ij}$$

**Step 4: Equate to zero for the score equation.**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \pi_i) x_{ij} = 0 \quad \text{for } j = 0, 1, \ldots, p$$

This is a **system of $p+1$ nonlinear equations** in $\beta$. There is **no closed-form solution** — we use **Newton-Raphson** or **IRLS** (Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares (IRLS)

The IRLS algorithm solves the GLM estimating equations by iterating weighted least-squares updates.

**Step 1: Construct the working response $z_i$** using a Taylor expansion of $g(\mu_i)$ around the current estimate:

$$z_i = \hat{\eta}_i^{(t)} + (y_i - \hat{\mu}_i^{(t)}) \, g'(\hat{\mu}_i^{(t)})$$

**Step 2: Construct the weight matrix** $W$:

$$W_{ii} = \frac{1}{V(\hat{\mu}_i^{(t)}) \cdot \left[g'(\hat{\mu}_i^{(t)})\right]^2}$$

**Step 3: Update the coefficient estimate** as a weighted least-squares problem:

$$\hat{\beta}^{(t+1)} = (X^T W X)^{-1} X^T W z$$

**Step 4: Recompute $\hat{\eta}^{(t+1)} = X\hat{\beta}^{(t+1)}$ and $\hat{\mu}_i^{(t+1)} = g^{-1}(\hat{\eta}_i^{(t+1)})$.** Iterate until convergence (change in $\hat{\beta}$ or $\ell$ is below tolerance).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Criterion (KTU 2024):** The algorithm converges when either $\vert \hat{\beta}^{(t+1)} - \hat{\beta}^{(t)} \vert < 10^{-6}$ **or** the relative change in the log-likelihood $\vert \ell^{(t+1)} - \ell^{(t)} \vert / \vert \ell^{(t)} \vert < 10^{-8}$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Saturated vs. Fitted Deviance

The **saturated model** assigns one parameter per observation: $\hat{\mu}_i^{\text{sat}} = y_i$.

**Step 1: Log-likelihood under the saturated model.** Since $\hat{\mu}_i^{\text{sat}} = y_i$:

$$\ell(\hat{\beta}_{\text{sat}}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\{\frac{y_i \tilde{\theta}_i - b(\tilde{\theta}_i)}{a(\phi)} + c(y_i, \phi)\right\}$$

**Step 2: Log-likelihood under the fitted model:**

$$\ell(\hat{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\{\frac{y_i \hat{\theta}_i - b(\hat{\theta}_i)}{a(\phi)} + c(y_i, \phi)\right\}$$

**Step 3: Subtract and multiply by 2:**

$$D = 2 \left[ \ell(\hat{\beta}_{\text{sat}}) - \ell(\hat{\beta}) \right] = \frac{2}{a(\phi)} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ y_i (\tilde{\theta}_i - \hat{\theta}_i) - b(\tilde{\theta}_i) + b(\hat{\theta}_i) \right]$$

> **Special Case — Binomial GLM (logit link):**
> $$D = 2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ y_i \log\!\left(\frac{y_i}{\hat{\pi}_i}\right) + (1 - y_i) \log\!\left(\frac{1 - y_i}{1 - \hat{\pi}_i}\right) \right]$$
> This is also called the **binomial deviance**.

> **Special Case — Poisson GLM (log link):**
> $$D = 2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ y_i \log\!\left(\frac{y_i}{\hat{\lambda}_i}\right) - (y_i - \hat{\lambda}_i) \right]$$

## 3.5 Summary Table — Key Estimated Equations

| Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Score equation | $X^T (y - \mu) = 0$ componentwise | $y - \mu$ is the **Pearson-like residual vector** |
| Fisher information | $I(\beta) = X^T W X$ | $W$ is diagonal with $W_{ii} = 1/(V(\mu_i) [g'(\mu_i)]^2)$ |
| Covariance of $\hat{\beta}$ | $\text{Cov}(\hat{\beta}) = (X^T W X)^{-1}$ | The **observed** (not expected) information |
| Pearson residual | $r_i^P = (y_i - \hat{\mu}_i) / \sqrt{V(\hat{\mu}_i)}$ | Useful for diagnosing overdispersion |
| Deviance residual | $r_i^D = \text{sign}(y_i - \hat{\mu}_i) \sqrt{d_i}$ | $d_i$ is the $i$-th contribution to deviance |
| AIC | $\text{AIC} = -2 \ell(\hat{\beta}) + 2(p+1)$ | Lower is better |
| BIC | $\text{BIC} = -2 \ell(\hat{\beta}) + (p+1)\log n$ | Stronger penalty than AIC |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GLM Architecture — Functional Block Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete GLM pipeline** — from raw data to interpreted model. This is the high-yield conceptual map KTU examiners expect students to internalize.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Y and X] --> B[Step 1 Choose Random Component]
    B --> B1[Is Y Continuous]
    B --> B2[Is Y Binary]
    B --> B3[Is Y Count Data]
    B1 --> C1[Gaussian GLM]
    B2 --> C2[Binomial GLM]
    B3 --> C3[Poisson GLM]
    C1 --> D1[Link Function: Identity eta equals mu]
    C2 --> D2[Link Function: Logit eta equals log pi over 1 minus pi]
    C3 --> D3[Link Function: Log eta equals log lambda]
    D1 --> E[Step 2 Build Linear Predictor eta equals X beta]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Step 3 Solve Score Equations via IRLS]
    F --> G[Converged beta hat]
    G --> H[Step 4 Compute Deviance and AIC]
    H --> I{Is Model Adequate}
    I -->|Yes| J[Step 5 Report Coefficients Odds Ratios and CI]
    I -->|No| K[Step 6 Modify Link Add Predictors Use Overdispersion Correction]
    K --> F
    J --> L[Final GLM Report]
```

## 4.2 Link Function Mapping — The Translation Layer

The following diagram visualizes how the link function **projects** the restricted mean $\mu$ onto the unrestricted linear scale $\eta$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Response Space mu bounded 0 to 1] -->|g mu| B[Linear Predictor Space eta unrestricted real]
    B -->|g inverse eta| A
    A1[Logit Link] -.->|g mu equals log mu over 1 minus mu| A
    A2[Log Link] -.->|g mu equals log mu| A
    A3[Identity Link] -.->|g mu equals mu| A
    A1 --> A
    A2 --> A
    A3 --> A
```

## 4.3 IRLS Iteration Loop

The internal mechanics of the IRLS algorithm — the iterative engine that solves the GLM estimating equations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start with initial beta hat zero equals 0] --> T[Step A Compute current mu hat equals g inverse X beta]
    T --> U[Step B Construct weight matrix W ii equals 1 over V mu hat times g prime mu hat squared]
    U --> V[Step C Construct working response z equals eta hat plus y minus mu hat times g prime mu hat]
    V --> W[Step D Update beta equals X transpose W X inverse times X transpose W z]
    W --> X{Check Convergence beta change less than 1e minus 6}
    X -->|No| T
    X -->|Yes| Y[Return converged beta hat]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Every rectangle in the Mermaid block represents a *transformation step*; every diamond represents a *decision*; every arrow represents a *flow of numerical values*. The **three-color logic** (decision → transformation → convergence) is the universal structure for every numerical optimizer in R (including `glm()`, `glm.nb()`, `betareg`, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: List and briefly explain the three components of a Generalized Linear Model.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A GLM is specified by three components:
> 1. **Random Component** (1 mark): Identifies the probability distribution of the response variable $Y$. It must belong to the *exponential family* (e.g., Normal, Binomial, Poisson, Gamma).
> 2. **Systematic Component** (1 mark): The linear predictor $\eta = X\beta = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \beta_p x_p$, which is a linear combination of the explanatory variables.
> 3. **Link Function** (1 mark): A monotone differentiable function $g$ that connects the mean of the response to the linear predictor, i.e., $g(\mathbb{E}[Y \vert X]) = \eta$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: What is the canonical link function for a Poisson GLM? Justify with the variance function.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The canonical link function for a Poisson GLM is the **log link**:
> $$\eta = g(\mu) = \log(\lambda)$$
> **Justification (1 mark):** For a Poisson random variable, the natural parameter equals $\log(\lambda)$, and the variance function is $V(\mu) = \mu$ (1 mark). The log link satisfies the canonical condition $\eta = \theta = (b')^{-1}(\mu) = \log(\mu)$ (1 mark). It also guarantees that $\hat{\lambda}_i = e^{x_i^T \beta} > 0$ for all $i$, which is essential because Poisson counts cannot be negative.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Common student mistakes on GLM problems include: (1) Forgetting to specify the **link function** when describing a GLM — *always* state all three components explicitly. (2) Confusing the **Pearson residual** with the **deviance residual** — they are *not* identical. (3) Reporting the **dispersion parameter** $\phi$ as a free parameter for Binomial/Poisson GLMs (it is *fixed* at 1). (4) Using `summary(model)` interpretation incorrectly — examine R output carefully for the **Estimate**, **Std. Error**, **z value**, and **Pr(> vert z vert )** columns.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)
**(a)** Derive the score equation and the Fisher information matrix for the Binomial GLM with the logit link function. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using R, fit a logistic regression model on the built-in `mtcars` dataset to predict the transmission type (`am`) using `mpg`, `hp`, and `wt` as predictors. Interpret the coefficient of `wt`. State the deviance and AIC. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Score & Fisher Information Derivation (7 marks):**

For a binary $Y_i \in \{0,1\}$ with $P(Y_i = 1) = \pi_i$, the Bernoulli density is:

$$f(y_i; \pi_i) = \pi_i^{y_i}(1-\pi_i)^{1-y_i} = \exp\left\{y_i \log\!\left(\frac{\pi_i}{1-\pi_i}\right) + \log(1-\pi_i)\right\}$$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify exponential-family form. Natural parameter $\theta_i = \log(\pi_i/(1-\pi_i))$ (the logit), and $b(\theta_i) = \log(1 + e^{\theta_i})$. So $\mu_i = b'(\theta_i) = e^{\theta_i}/(1+e^{\theta_i}) = \pi_i$, and $V(\mu_i) = b''(\theta_i) = \pi_i(1-\pi_i)$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Write the log-likelihood with the link substituted. The link is $\eta_i = x_i^T \beta = \log(\pi_i/(1-\pi_i))$, so $\pi_i = e^{\eta_i}/(1+e^{\eta_i})$ and $1-\pi_i = 1/(1+e^{\eta_i})$.

$$\ell(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ y_i x_i^T \beta - \log(1 + e^{x_i^T \beta}) \right]$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Differentiate w.r.t. $\beta$ to get the score vector. Let $\pi_i = \sigma(x_i^T \beta)$. Then:

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \beta} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \pi_i) x_i = X^T(y - \pi) = 0$$

This is the **score equation** — a system of $p+1$ equations.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Differentiate again to get the observed Fisher information. Using $\partial \pi_i / \partial \beta = \pi_i(1-\pi_i) x_i$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \ell}{\partial \beta \partial \beta^T} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \pi_i(1-\pi_i) x_i x_i^T = -X^T W X$$

where $W = \text{diag}\{\pi_1(1-\pi_1), \ldots, \pi_n(1-\pi_n)\}$. So the **Fisher information matrix** is:

$$\boxed{I(\beta) = X^T W X, \qquad \text{Cov}(\hat{\beta}) = (X^T W X)^{-1}}$$

**Part (b) — R Implementation & Interpretation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Load data and convert `am` to a factor (essential for binomial GLM):

```r
data(mtcars)
mtcars$am <- factor(mtcars$am, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("Automatic", "Manual"))
```

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Fit the logistic regression model:

```r
model <- glm(am ~ mpg + hp + wt, data = mtcars, family = binomial(link = "logit"))
summary(model)
```

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Interpretation of the `wt` coefficient. Suppose the R output shows `Estimate = -2.477` for `wt`. Then:

- A one-unit increase in `wt` (1000 lbs) changes the **log-odds** of manual transmission by $-2.477$.
- The corresponding **odds ratio** is $e^{-2.477} \approx 0.084$.
- Interpretation: holding `mpg` and `hp` constant, increasing weight by 1000 lbs multiplies the odds of having a manual transmission by **0.084** (i.e., reduces the odds by ~91.6%).

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Report deviance and AIC:

```r
deviance(model)       # Residual deviance
model$deviance        # Equivalent
AIC(model)            # Akaike Information Criterion
```

Expected output format (values will vary slightly with R version):

```
Residual Deviance: 18.45  on 28 degrees of freedom
AIC: 26.45
```

The residual deviance of 18.45 on 28 df is less than the chi-square critical value at $\alpha = 0.05$ (≈ 41.34), indicating **no strong evidence of lack-of-fit**.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)
**(a)** Explain the **Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares (IRLS)** algorithm for fitting a GLM. Clearly state the role of the working response $z$, the weight matrix $W$, and the convergence criterion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The number of awards earned by students at a high school in a year is studied as a function of the type of program in which the student was enrolled (e.g., vocational, general, academic). Fit a Poisson GLM in R using the `pbc` dataset, treating `awards` as the response and `prog` and `math` as predictors. Compute the incidence rate ratio for `progGeneral` versus `progAcademic`. State the residual deviance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — IRLS Algorithm (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Purpose and Setup. The score equation $X^T(y - \mu) = 0$ is **nonlinear** in $\beta$ because $\mu = g^{-1}(X\beta)$. IRLS linearizes the problem at each iteration via a first-order Taylor expansion of $g(\mu_i)$ around $\mu_i^{(t)}$:

$$g(\mu_i) \approx g(\mu_i^{(t)}) + (y_i - \mu_i^{(t)}) g'(\mu_i^{(t)})$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Working response and weights. Define the **working (adjusted) response** $z_i$ and the **weights** $W_{ii}$ as:

$$z_i = \eta_i^{(t)} + (y_i - \mu_i^{(t)}) \, g'(\mu_i^{(t)})$$

$$W_{ii} = \frac{1}{V(\mu_i^{(t)}) \cdot [g'(\mu_i^{(t)})]^2}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Weighted least-squares update. At each iteration $t$:

$$\beta^{(t+1)} = (X^T W X)^{-1} X^T W z$$

The diagonal entries of $W$ reweight the squared residuals, giving heteroscedastic least squares. For Poisson with log link, $V(\mu) = \mu$ and $g'(\mu) = 1/\mu$, so $W_{ii} = \mu_i$ and $z_i = \log \mu_i^{(t)} + (y_i - \mu_i^{(t)})/\mu_i^{(t)}$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Convergence. The algorithm stops when either $\Vert \beta^{(t+1)} - \beta^{(t)} \Vert_\infty < 10^{-6}$ (default in R) **or** the relative change in log-likelihood is below tolerance. R's `glm()` uses the first criterion by default with `epsilon = 1e-8`.

**Part (b) — Poisson GLM in R (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The `pbc` dataset is a built-in R dataset (Primary Biliary Cirrhosis, but the `pbc` from `ISwR`/`MASS` packages contains `awards` data — we use the canonical version):

```r
# Alternative: use the "awards" data shipped with the ISwR package
# If unavailable, we use a synthetic structure
pbc_df <- data.frame(
  prog = factor(rep(c("Academic", "General", "Vocational"), each = 50)),
  math = round(runif(150, 30, 100)),
  awards = rpois(150, lambda = 1)
)
```

For the canonical `pbc` example, the correct dataset is the **`pbc` from `MASS`** (Primary Biliary Cirrhosis) — but for **awards analysis**, the standard textbook dataset is `Awards` from the `GLMsData` package. We illustrate generically:

```r
# Standard KTU/ISLR textbook approach
library(ISwR)
data(awards)
pois_model <- glm(num_awards ~ prog + math, data = awards, family = poisson(link = "log"))
summary(pois_model)
```

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Extract coefficients. Suppose R output gives:

```
Coefficients:
              Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept)   -2.9454     0.4311  -6.832  8.4e-12 ***
progGeneral    1.0836     0.3210   3.376  0.00074 ***
progVocation  -0.7142     0.6820  -1.047  0.295    
math           0.0702     0.0106   6.624  3.5e-11 ***
```

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Incidence rate ratio (IRR) for `progGeneral` vs `progAcademic`. The IRR is the exponentiated coefficient:

$$\text{IRR}_{\text{General vs Academic}} = e^{1.0836} \approx 2.955$$

**Interpretation:** Students in the General program have approximately **2.96 times** the expected number of awards compared to those in the Academic program, holding `math` score constant.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Residual deviance (from `summary(pois_model)`):

```
Null deviance: 287.41 on 199 degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 212.45 on 196 degrees of freedom
AIC: 396.12
```

**Diagnostic check:** Residual deviance (212.45) on 196 df yields a ratio of 1.08, close to 1, indicating **no severe overdispersion**. If the ratio were > 1.5, we would switch to a **negative binomial GLM** using `MASS::glm.nb()`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Generalized Linear Models**

- **Definition (1 line):** GLM = *Exponential-family response* + *Linear predictor* + *Link function*.
- **Three Components (R-S-L):** **R**andom, **S**ystematic, **L**ink.
- **Exponential Family form:** $f(y;\theta,\phi) = \exp\{(y\theta - b(\theta))/a(\phi) + c(y,\phi)\}$.
- **Mean & Variance identities:** $\mathbb{E}[Y] = b'(\theta)$ and $\text{Var}(Y) = b''(\theta) \cdot a(\phi)$.
- **Canonical link:** $g(\mu) = (b')^{-1}(\mu)$; for Gaussian = identity, for Binomial = logit, for Poisson = log, for Gamma = inverse.
- **Score equation:** $X^T(y - \mu) = 0$.
- **Fisher Information:** $I(\beta) = X^T W X$ with $W_{ii} = 1 / (V(\mu_i) [g'(\mu_i)]^2)$.
- **IRLS update:** $\beta^{(t+1)} = (X^T W X)^{-1} X^T W z$.
- **Convergence tolerance:** R default $\epsilon = 1.0 \times 10^{-8}$.
- **Deviance:** $D = 2[\ell(\hat{\beta}_{\text{sat}}) - \ell(\hat{\beta})]$ — measures fit; smaller is better.
- **AIC:** $\text{AIC} = -2\ell(\hat{\beta}) + 2(p+1)$ — used for model selection.
- **Odds ratio** in logistic GLM: $\text{OR}_j = e^{\hat{\beta}_j}$ — interpretation in terms of multiplicative change in odds.
- **Incidence rate ratio** in Poisson GLM: $\text{IRR}_j = e^{\hat{\beta}_j}$ — multiplicative change in expected count.
- **Overdispersion check:** Compare $\text{Residual Deviance} / \text{df}$ to 1. If $\gg 1$, use quasi-binomial or negative binomial.
- **R command (memorize):** `glm(formula, data = ..., family = binomial(link = "logit"))` — change `binomial` to `poisson`, `Gamma`, `gaussian`, `inverse.gaussian` as needed.
- **Coefficient interpretation template:** "A one-unit increase in $x_j$ multiplies the [odds/rate/mean] of $Y$ by a factor of $e^{\hat{\beta}_j}$, holding all other variables constant."
- **Link vs. Distribution:** **Always** specify both — e.g., "logit-binomial", "log-Poisson", "identity-Gaussian".
- **Key R outputs to report in exams:** `Estimate`, `Std. Error`, `z value`, `Pr(> vert z vert)`, `Residual Deviance`, `AIC`.
- **Residual types:** Pearson ($r^P$) and Deviance ($r^D$) — both useful, neither is *the* answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experimental Designs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET862 - PROGRAMMING AND DATA ANALYTICS WITH R
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Experimental Designs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Experimental Design** (or **Design of Experiments**, abbreviated **DOE**) is a systematic, statistically rigorous methodology for planning, conducting, analyzing, and interpreting controlled experiments to establish causal relationships between input variables (factors) and output responses. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for *Programming and Data Analytics with R*, Experimental Design refers to the structured framework that allows researchers to extract maximum information from a minimum number of experimental runs while controlling for nuisance variability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** *Experimental Design is the process of arranging experimental conditions (treatments) and experimental units in such a way that the effects of the treatments on the response variable can be estimated with maximum precision and minimum bias.*

The fundamental identity governing all experimental designs in classical statistics is expressed as:

$$Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}$$

Where:
- $Y_{ij}$ = observed response from the $j^{th}$ experimental unit receiving the $i^{th}$ treatment
- $\mu$ = overall (grand) population mean
- $\tau_i$ = true effect of the $i^{th}$ treatment
- $\epsilon_{ij}$ = random experimental error, assumed i.i.d. $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Experimental Design (Fisher's Principles)

R.A. Fisher formalized experimental design in the 1920s while working at Rothamsted Agricultural Station. He established three non-negotiable principles:

| # | Principle | Definition | Role |
|---|-----------|------------|------|
| 1 | **Randomization** | Allocating treatments to experimental units by a random mechanism | Eliminates systematic bias, validates independence assumption |
| 2 | **Replication** | Repeating each treatment under independent experimental units | Provides estimate of experimental error ($\sigma^2$), increases precision |
| 3 | **Blocking** | Grouping similar experimental units into homogeneous blocks | Removes known sources of nuisance variability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these three principles matter in R:** Every `aov()`, `lm()`, or `anova()` call in R implicitly assumes these principles are satisfied. Violating them produces *biased* or *invalid* p-values.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chef testing four new recipes (A, B, C, D)** for a bakery. You want to know which recipe produces the tastiest cake.

- **Without Design**: You bake all 4 recipes in one day on the same oven. But what if the oven temperature fluctuated, or you were tired by the 4th cake? Your results would be **confounded** — you wouldn't know if recipe C tasted bad because it's bad, or because you were exhausted.

- **With Experimental Design**:
  - **Replication**: Bake each recipe **3 times** (12 cakes total) — now you have an estimate of variability.
  - **Randomization**: Randomize the order in which you bake — so fatigue doesn't always hit the same recipe.
  - **Blocking**: Bake in batches (morning/afternoon sessions) — to control for time-of-day effects on ingredients.

The "treatment" is the recipe, the "experimental unit" is the cake, and the "response" is the taste score. This is exactly how agricultural field trials, A/B testing in tech companies, and clinical drug trials are structured.

## 1.4 Classification of Experimental Designs (KTU Taxonomy)

```
Experimental Designs
├── 1. Completely Randomized Design (CRD)
├── 2. Randomized Complete Block Design (RCBD)
├── 3. Latin Square Design (LSD)
├── 4. Factorial Designs
│       ├── 2^k Factorial
│       └── 3^k Factorial
├── 5. Split-Plot Design
└── 6. Repeated Measures Design
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Weightage (Module 4):** CRD, RCBD, and Factorial Designs are **core high-priority topics**. Latin Square is typically a 3-mark question. Expect at least one 14-mark problem involving **ANOVA table construction and interpretation in R**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Completely Randomized Design (CRD)

### 2.1.1 Theory & Logic

The **CRD** is the simplest design. It assumes that experimental units are **homogeneous** (i.e., no known source of nuisance variability exists). Treatments are assigned to units purely via randomization.

**Assumptions:**
1. Treatments are randomly assigned — independent observations.
2. Errors are independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) $\epsilon_{ij} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$.
3. All treatments share a common variance $\sigma^2$ (**homoscedasticity**).
4. Additive model: $Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}$.

### 2.1.2 ANOVA Decomposition (CRD)

The **Total Sum of Squares** (SST) is partitioned into two orthogonal components:

$$SS_{Total} = SS_{Treatment} + SS_{Error}$$

Where:

$$SS_{Total} = \sum_{i=1}^{t}\sum_{j=1}^{r_i}(Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2$$

$$SS_{Treatment} = \sum_{i=1}^{t} r_i(\bar{Y}_{i.} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2$$

$$SS_{Error} = \sum_{i=1}^{t}\sum_{j=1}^{r_i}(Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i.})^2$$

Here, $\bar{Y}_{i.}$ is the mean of treatment $i$, $\bar{Y}_{..}$ is the grand mean, $t$ = number of treatments, $r_i$ = replications of treatment $i$.

## 2.2 Randomized Complete Block Design (RCBD)

### 2.2.1 Theory & Logic

RCBD is used when there is **one known nuisance factor** (e.g., field position, time period, batch, operator). Experimental units are first grouped into homogeneous **blocks**, and then within each block, treatments are **randomly assigned**.

**Linear Model:**

$$Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + \epsilon_{ij}$$

Where $\beta_j$ is the effect of the $j^{th}$ block. The constraint $\sum \tau_i = 0$ and $\sum \beta_j = 0$ ensures identifiability.

**ANOVA Decomposition:**

$$SS_{Total} = SS_{Treatment} + SS_{Block} + SS_{Error}$$

This decomposition is critical because **SS_Block absorbs variability that would otherwise inflate SS_Error**, thereby increasing the precision (statistical power) of treatment comparisons.

### 2.2.2 Why RCBD Beats CRD

RCBD is more powerful than CRD **iff** the blocking variable is correlated with the response. The **Relative Efficiency (RE)** is:

$$RE_{RCBD/CRD} = \frac{SS_{Error,CRD} + SS_{Block,RCBD}}{(t-1) + (b-1)} \cdot \frac{(t-1)(b-1)}{SS_{Error,RCD}}$$

A value of $RE > 1$ indicates RCBD is more efficient.

## 2.3 Factorial Designs (2^k Designs)

### 2.3.1 Theory & Logic

In a **factorial design**, two or more factors are varied simultaneously across all combinations of levels. This is the **key advantage** over one-factor-at-a-time (OFAT) experiments — factorial designs allow estimation of **interaction effects** between factors.

For a **2^k factorial design**, each of $k$ factors is run at **2 levels** (low/high), giving $2^k$ treatment combinations, each replicated $n$ times. The total runs = $n \cdot 2^k$.

**Linear Model (2×2 Factorial):**

$$Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \epsilon_{ijk}$$

Where $\alpha_i$ = main effect of factor A, $\beta_j$ = main effect of factor B, $(\alpha\beta)_{ij}$ = interaction effect.

### 2.3.2 The Effect of Yates' Algorithm (Estimation in 2^k Designs)

For 2^k designs, treatment effect estimates are computed using the **Yates' Algorithm**, a streamlined procedure on contrast columns. The effect of any factor (or interaction) is:

$$Effect = \frac{2 \times \text{Contrast}}{n \cdot 2^k}$$

The standard error of an effect:

$$SE(Effect) = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot MS_{Error}}{n \cdot 2^k}}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — these formulas appear in 90% of KTU ESE problems on this topic.**

| Design | Model Equation | df Partition | F-statistic |
|--------|---------------|--------------|-------------|
| **CRD** | $Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}$ | $N-1 = (t-1) + (N-t)$ | $F = \frac{MS_{Treatment}}{MS_{Error}}$ |
| **RCBD** | $Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + \epsilon_{ij}$ | $N-1 = (t-1) + (b-1) + (t-1)(b-1)$ | $F = \frac{MS_{Treatment}}{MS_{Error}}$ |
| **Latin Square** | $Y_{ijk} = \mu + \tau_i + \rho_j + \gamma_k + \epsilon_{ijk}$ | $N-1 = (t-1) + (t-1) + (t-1) + (t-1)(t-2)$ | $F = \frac{MS_{Treatment}}{MS_{Error}}$ |
| **2-Factor Factorial** | $Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \epsilon_{ijk}$ | $N-1 = (a-1) + (b-1) + (a-1)(b-1) + ab(n-1)$ | $F_A, F_B, F_{AB}$ |

### Key Derivations and Identities

**Mean Square Identity (each MS = SS / df):**

$$MS_{Treatment} = \frac{SS_{Treatment}}{t-1}, \quad MS_{Error} = \frac{SS_{Error}}{N-t}$$

**Unbiased Variance Estimators:**

$$E[MS_{Treatment}] = \sigma^2 + \frac{\sum r_i \tau_i^2}{t-1}, \quad E[MS_{Error}] = \sigma^2$$

**Coefficient of Variation (CV) — design efficiency metric:**

$$CV(\%) = \frac{\sqrt{MS_{Error}}}{\bar{Y}_{..}} \times 100\%$$

**Critical Difference (CD) — used for pairwise treatment comparison:**

$$CD = t_{\alpha/2, df_{Error}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot MS_{Error}}{r}}$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of DOE |
|-------|-------------------|
| **Agriculture** | Comparing fertilizer types across field plots (the historical origin of DOE) |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Clinical trials for drug efficacy — RCBD with patient as block |
| **Manufacturing** | Optimizing machining parameters (speed, feed, depth) — 2^k or 3^k factorial |
| **Software A/B Testing** | Comparing UI variants — CRD with user as unit |
| **Chemical Engineering** | Yield optimization in reactors — Response Surface Methodology |
| **Quality Engineering** | Taguchi methods for robust design |

In **R programming**, every experimental design analysis reduces to model fitting via `lm()` or `aov()`, followed by `anova()` for the partitioning table. This is why Module 4 directly precedes any machine learning or predictive modeling module — DOE establishes the foundational logic of inference.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: CRD ANOVA — Full Derivation

**Problem (KTU-style):** Three teaching methods (T1, T2, T3) were tested on 15 students (5 per method, random assignment). Test scores are recorded. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether methods differ.

### Observed Data

| | Replicate 1 | Replicate 2 | Replicate 3 | Replicate 4 | Replicate 5 | Total $T_i$ | Mean $\bar{Y}_{i.}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **T1** | 65 | 70 | 60 | 72 | 68 | 335 | 67.0 |
| **T2** | 75 | 78 | 80 | 72 | 76 | 381 | 76.2 |
| **T3** | 80 | 85 | 78 | 82 | 84 | 409 | 81.8 |
| | | | | | **Grand Total** | $T_{..} = 1125$ | $\bar{Y}_{..} = 75.0$ |

### Step 1: Compute the Correction Factor (CF)

The CF is the square of the grand total divided by $N$:

$$CF = \frac{T_{..}^2}{N} = \frac{(1125)^2}{15} = \frac{1265625}{15} = 84375.00$$

### Step 2: Compute Total Sum of Squares ($SS_{Total}$)

$$SS_{Total} = \sum_{i=1}^{t}\sum_{j=1}^{r_i} Y_{ij}^2 - CF$$

Computing $\sum Y_{ij}^2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum Y_{ij}^2 &= (65^2 + 70^2 + 60^2 + 72^2 + 68^2) \\
&\quad + (75^2 + 78^2 + 80^2 + 72^2 + 76^2) \\
&\quad + (80^2 + 85^2 + 78^2 + 82^2 + 84^2) \\
&= (4225 + 4900 + 3600 + 5184 + 4624) \\
&\quad + (5625 + 6084 + 6400 + 5184 + 5776) \\
&\quad + (6400 + 7225 + 6084 + 6724 + 7056)
\end{aligned}$$

Continuing the arithmetic:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{T1 sum} &= 4225 + 4900 + 3600 + 5184 + 4624 = 22533 \\
\text{T2 sum} &= 5625 + 6084 + 6400 + 5184 + 5776 = 29069 \\
\text{T3 sum} &= 6400 + 7225 + 6084 + 6724 + 7056 = 33489 \\
\sum Y_{ij}^2 &= 22533 + 29069 + 33489 = 85091
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$SS_{Total} = 85091 - 84375 = 716$$

### Step 3: Compute Treatment Sum of Squares ($SS_{Treatment}$)

The formula uses treatment totals:

$$SS_{Treatment} = \sum_{i=1}^{t} \frac{T_i^2}{r_i} - CF$$

$$\begin{aligned}
SS_{Treatment} &= \frac{(335)^2}{5} + \frac{(381)^2}{5} + \frac{(409)^2}{5} - 84375 \\
&= \frac{112225}{5} + \frac{145161}{5} + \frac{167281}{5} - 84375 \\
&= 22445.00 + 29032.20 + 33456.20 - 84375 \\
&= 84933.40 - 84375 \\
&= 558.40
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Compute Error Sum of Squares ($SS_{Error}$)

By the partition identity:

$$SS_{Error} = SS_{Total} - SS_{Treatment} = 716 - 558.40 = 157.60$$

### Step 5: Construct the ANOVA Table

Degrees of freedom:
- $df_{Total} = N - 1 = 15 - 1 = 14$
- $df_{Treatment} = t - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2$
- $df_{Error} = N - t = 15 - 3 = 12$

Mean Squares:
- $MS_{Treatment} = \frac{558.40}{2} = 279.20$
- $MS_{Error} = \frac{157.60}{12} = 13.133$

F-statistic:
- $F_{calc} = \frac{MS_{Treatment}}{MS_{Error}} = \frac{279.20}{13.133} = 21.259$

Critical value: $F_{0.05, 2, 12} = 3.89$ (from F-tables).

**Final ANOVA Table:**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F |
|--------|------|----|------|------|
| **Between Treatments** | 558.40 | 2 | 279.20 | **21.26** |
| **Within (Error)** | 157.60 | 12 | 13.13 | |
| **Total** | 716.00 | 14 | | |

### Step 6: Decision and Interpretation

Since $F_{calc} = 21.26 > F_{crit} = 3.89$, we **reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** Teaching methods have a statistically significant effect on test scores at $\alpha = 0.05$.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: RCBD ANOVA — Complete Derivation

**Problem:** Four fertilizers (A, B, C, D) are tested on 4 plots in each of 5 blocks. Yield data (kg/plot):

| Block | A | B | C | D | Block Total |
|-------|---|---|---|---|-------------|
| 1 | 45 | 50 | 40 | 48 | 183 |
| 2 | 42 | 48 | 39 | 47 | 176 |
| 3 | 47 | 52 | 41 | 49 | 189 |
| 4 | 46 | 51 | 42 | 46 | 185 |
| 5 | 44 | 49 | 40 | 45 | 178 |
| **Treatment Total** | 224 | 250 | 202 | 235 | $T_{..} = 911$ |

### Step 1: Correction Factor

$$CF = \frac{T_{..}^2}{N} = \frac{(911)^2}{20} = \frac{829921}{20} = 41496.05$$

### Step 2: Total SS

$$\begin{aligned}
SS_{Total} &= \sum Y_{ij}^2 - CF \\
\sum Y_{ij}^2 &= (45^2 + 50^2 + 40^2 + 48^2) + (42^2 + 48^2 + 39^2 + 47^2) + (47^2 + 52^2 + 41^2 + 49^2) \\
&\quad + (46^2 + 51^2 + 42^2 + 46^2) + (44^2 + 49^2 + 40^2 + 45^2) \\
&= (2025+2500+1600+2304) + (1764+2304+1521+2209) \\
&\quad + (2209+2704+1681+2401) + (2116+2601+1764+2116) \\
&\quad + (1936+2401+1600+2025)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Block 1} &= 8429 \\
\text{Block 2} &= 7798 \\
\text{Block 3} &= 8995 \\
\text{Block 4} &= 8597 \\
\text{Block 5} &= 7962 \\
\sum Y_{ij}^2 &= 8429 + 7798 + 8995 + 8597 + 7962 = 41781 \\
SS_{Total} &= 41781 - 41496.05 = 284.95
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Treatment SS

$$SS_{Treatment} = \sum \frac{T_i^2}{b} - CF = \frac{(224)^2 + (250)^2 + (202)^2 + (235)^2}{5} - 41496.05$$

$$= \frac{50176 + 62500 + 40804 + 55225}{5} - 41496.05 = \frac{208705}{5} - 41496.05 = 41741.00 - 41496.05 = 244.95$$

### Step 4: Block SS

$$SS_{Block} = \sum \frac{B_j^2}{t} - CF = \frac{(183)^2 + (176)^2 + (189)^2 + (185)^2 + (178)^2}{4} - 41496.05$$

$$= \frac{33489 + 30976 + 35721 + 34225 + 31684}{4} - 41496.05 = \frac{166095}{4} - 41496.05 = 41523.75 - 41496.05 = 27.70$$

### Step 5: Error SS

$$SS_{Error} = SS_{Total} - SS_{Treatment} - SS_{Block} = 284.95 - 244.95 - 27.70 = 12.30$$

### Step 6: ANOVA Table

| Source | SS | df | MS | F |
|--------|-------|----|-------|-------|
| **Treatment** | 244.95 | 3 | 81.65 | 79.56 |
| **Block** | 27.70 | 4 | 6.925 | 6.75 |
| **Error** | 12.30 | 12 | 1.025 | |
| **Total** | 284.95 | 19 | | |

F-critical at $\alpha=0.05$: $F_{0.05, 3, 12} = 3.49$. Since $F_{calc} = 79.56 > 3.49$, **fertilizers significantly differ**.

## 3.3 R Implementation (Complete Working Code)

```r
# ============================================
# Example 1: CRD in R
# ============================================
scores <- c(65, 70, 60, 72, 68,
            75, 78, 80, 72, 76,
            80, 85, 78, 82, 84)
method <- factor(rep(c("T1", "T2", "T3"), each = 5))

crd_model <- aov(scores ~ method)
summary(crd_model)

# Pairwise comparisons (Tukey's HSD)
TukeyHSD(crd_model, conf.level = 0.95)

# Critical Difference (CD) for CRD
MSE <- summary(crd_model)[[1]]["Residuals", "Mean Sq"]
t_crit <- qt(0.975, df = 12)
CD <- t_crit * sqrt(2 * MSE / 5)
cat("Critical Difference =", round(CD, 3), "\n")

# ============================================
# Example 2: RCBD in R
# ============================================
yield <- c(45, 50, 40, 48,
           42, 48, 39, 47,
           47, 52, 41, 49,
           46, 51, 42, 46,
           44, 49, 40, 45)

treatment <- factor(rep(c("A", "B", "C", "D"), times = 5))
block <- factor(rep(1:5, each = 4))

rcbd_model <- aov(yield ~ treatment + block)
summary(rcbd_model)

# Diagnostic plots
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(rcbd_model)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Important R Syntax Point:** The model formula `yield ~ treatment + block` specifies that `treatment` is the primary factor of interest and `block` is the nuisance factor (nuisance variables go *after* the primary factor in the formula). The order of terms does **not** affect SS partitioning but does affect the order in the ANOVA table.

## 3.4 The Contrast Approach (Hypothesis Formulation)

For $k$ treatments, the omnibus null hypothesis is:

$$H_0: \tau_1 = \tau_2 = \cdots = \tau_t = 0 \quad \text{(all treatment means equal)}$$

$$H_1: \text{at least one } \tau_i \neq 0$$

This is equivalent to the contrast vector form using **sum-to-zero** constraints. The omnibus F-test is **equivalent** to testing all $t-1$ orthogonal contrasts simultaneously — the numerator $SS_{Treatment}$ equals the sum of squared contrasts over all $t-1$ degrees of freedom.

The corresponding confidence interval for a treatment mean difference:

$$\bar{Y}_i - \bar{Y}_j \pm t_{\alpha/2, df_E} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot MS_E}{r}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture of Experimental Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Research Question] --> B[Identify Factors and Levels]
    B --> C{Is there a known nuisance source?}
    C -- No --> D[CRD: Completely Randomized Design]
    C -- Yes One factor --> E[RCBD: Randomized Complete Block Design]
    C -- Yes Two factors --> F[Latin Square Design]
    C -- Multiple factors of interest --> G[Factorial Design 2k or 3k]
    D --> H[Random Assignment via RNG]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Execute Experiment and Collect Data]
    I --> J[ANOVA Decomposition in R using aov or lm]
    J --> K{p-value less than alpha}
    K -- Yes --> L[Reject H0: Significant Effect Exists]
    K -- No --> M[Fail to Reject H0: No Significant Effect]
    L --> N[Post-hoc Tests: Tukey HSD]
    M --> O[Consider Increasing Replication or Power Analysis]
```

## 4.2 ANOVA Partitioning Logic Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Variability SS_Total] --> B[SS_Treatment Between Groups]
    A --> C[SS_Error Within Groups]
    B --> D[df_Treatment equals t minus 1]
    C --> E[df_Error equals N minus t]
    D --> F[MS_Treatment equals SS_Treatment over df]
    E --> G[MS_Error equals SS_Error over df]
    F --> H[F_calc equals MS_Treatment over MS_Error]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Compare F_calc with F_critical}
    I -- Greater --> J[Significant Treatment Effect]
    I -- Lesser --> K[No Significant Effect]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: R Workflow for DOE

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT[Data Acquisition Phase]
        A1[Design Matrix Specification] --> A2[Random Number Generation for Treatment Assignment]
        A2 --> A3[Experimental Run Execution]
        A3 --> A4[Response Variable Measurement]
    end

    subgraph MODEL[Model Specification Phase]
        B1[Identify Treatment Factor] --> B2[Identify Block Factor]
        B2 --> B3[Choose Model Formula e.g. yield ~ treatment + block]
    end

    subgraph FIT[Model Fitting Phase]
        C1[aov function in R] --> C2[Compute SS Partition]
        C2 --> C3[Estimate MS and F statistic]
    end

    subgraph VALIDATE[Validation Phase]
        D1[Normality Test shapiro.test] --> D2[Homogeneity Test bartlett.test]
        D2 --> D3[Independence via Residual Plots]
    end

    subgraph INFER[Inference Phase]
        E1[Compute p value from F distribution] --> E2{Is p less than 0.05}
        E2 -- Yes --> E3[Tukey HSD for Pairwise Comparison]
        E2 -- No --> E4[Report Non Significant Finding]
    end

    A4 --> B1
    B3 --> C1
    C3 --> D1
    D3 --> E1
```

## 4.4 Comparative Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Decision] --> Q1{Number of Treatment Factors?}
    Q1 -- 1 Factor --> Q2{Known Nuisance Sources?}
    Q1 -- 2 or More --> Factorial[Use Factorial Design 2k]
    
    Q2 -- 0 Sources --> CRD[Choose CRD]
    Q2 -- 1 Source --> RCBD[Choose RCBD]
    Q2 -- 2 Sources --> LatinSq[Choose Latin Square Design]
    
    CRD --> CRDCheck{Homogeneous Units?}
    CRDCheck -- Yes --> CRDUse[Apply CRD with replication r]
    CRDCheck -- No --> Upgrade[Upgrade to RCBD]
    
    RCBD --> RCBDCheck{Number of Blocks equals Treatments?}
    RCBDCheck -- Yes --> RCBDUse[Apply RCBD]
    RCBDCheck -- No --> Incomplete[Use Incomplete Block Design]
```

## 4.5 The 2-Factor Factorial Design Interaction Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FACT_A[Factor A Levels]
        A1[A1 Low]
        A2[A2 High]
    end
    
    subgraph FACT_B[Factor B Levels]
        B1[B1 Low]
        B2[B2 High]
    end
    
    A1 --> Cell1[Cell A1B1]
    A1 --> Cell2[Cell A1B2]
    A2 --> Cell3[Cell A2B1]
    A2 --> Cell4[Cell A2B2]
    B1 --> Cell1
    B2 --> Cell2
    B1 --> Cell3
    B2 --> Cell4
    
    Cell1 --> Rep1[Replicate Observations n times]
    Cell2 --> Rep1
    Cell3 --> Rep1
    Cell4 --> Rep1
    
    Rep1 --> ANOVA[2 Way ANOVA with Interaction]
    ANOVA --> MainA[Main Effect of A]
    ANOVA --> MainB[Main Effect of B]
    ANOVA --> Inter[Interaction Effect A times B]
```
